ground_truth_moral: They who act without sufficient thought, will often fall into unsuspected danger

summaries.direct_moral: Do not mistake what is not what for what it seems to be.
summaries.cot_proverb: The mind's own eye is often deceived by its own assumptions.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Mistaking due to rigid preconception leads to self-inflicted consequence.

fable_text: A sow and a dog were arguing about their litters. The dog said that she had the easiest delivery of all the four-footed animals but the sow responded, 'Be that as it may, the puppies you give birth to are blind!'
ground_truth_moral: Enterprise is not to be judged in terms of its speed but its outcome.

summaries.direct_moral: Even in disagreement, one's perceived advantage does not negate the inherent flaws of one's offspring.
summaries.cot_proverb: The ease of birth does not guarantee the quality of the offspring.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The inherent value or ease of a process does not negate the existence of inherent, unavoidable flaws or limitations within the result.

fable_text: While digging up dead people's bones, a dog uncovered a treasure. This outraged the spirits of the dead, and the dog was punished for his sacrilege by being stricken with a desire for wealth. Thus, while the dog stood there guarding the treasure, he took no thought for food and wasted away from starvation. A vulture perched above him is rumoured to have said, 'O you dog, you deserve to die, since all of a sudden you began to crave the wealth of a king even though you were conceived in the gutter and were raised on a dungheap!'
ground_truth_moral: Greed can lead to ruin.

summaries.direct_moral: Deserving a great fortune can lead to ruin if it is undeserved.
summaries.cot_proverb: The desire for ill-gotten gain leads to ruin, even when the pursuit is misguided.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Unjust reward breeds destructive greed.

fable_text: A dog was sleeping in front of the barn when a wolf noticed him lying there. The wolf was ready to devour the dog, but the dog begged the wolf to let him go for the time being. 'At the moment I am thin and scrawny,' said the dog, 'but my owners are about to celebrate a wedding, so if you let me go now, I'll get fattened up and you can make a meal of me later on.' The wolf trusted the dog and let him go. When he came back a few days later, he saw the dog sleeping on the roof. The wolf shouted to the dog, reminding him of their agreement, but the dog simply said, 'Wolf, if you ever catch me sleeping in front of the barn again, don't wait for a wedding!'
ground_truth_moral: Take what you can get when you can get it.

summaries.direct_moral: A temporary favor granted by a predator can be repaid with a clever, future demand.
summaries.cot_proverb: The promise made under duress is often forgotten when the immediate danger passes.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Trust is often exploited when self-interest is involved.

fable_text: As he chased after a wolf, the dog capered with pleasure at how quickly he was able to run, revelling in his own strength. In fact, the dog actually imagined that the wolf was running away from him because of his superior prowess. But then the wolf turned around and said to the dog, 'I am not running away from you! I'm just afraid of being chased down by your master.'
ground_truth_moral: Do not take pride in the good qualities that actually belong to someone else.

summaries.direct_moral: Blind pride often leads to misunderstanding and misplaced confidence.
summaries.cot_proverb: The shadow of false admiration.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Blind admiration for personal achievement often masks a deeper, more complex reality of interdependence and mutual vulnerability.

fable_text: A gardener had a dog who had fallen into a well. The gardener lowered himself down into the well to pull the dog out, but the dog only gave the man a nasty bite, thinking that the man intended to plunge him even deeper into the water. After the dog had bitten him, the gardener said, 'It serves me right for making such an effort to pull you out of the well, when all you can do is attack me.'
ground_truth_moral: Benefits are refused by those who don't understand them.

summaries.direct_moral: Attempting to rescue another can result in being harmed by the very thing you seek to save.
summaries.cot_proverb: When one seeks to save another, be wary that the saved may seek to harm the savior.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of rescue can backfire when the rescuer's intent is misinterpreted as an aggressive threat, leading to reciprocal harm.

fable_text: A dog was running after a hare and when he caught him, he would alternately bite the hare and then lick the blood that flowed from the wound. The hare thought that the dog was kissing him, so he said, 'You should either embrace me as a friend, or bite me like an enemy.'
ground_truth_moral: No one can be a friend if you know not whether to trust or distrust him.

summaries.direct_moral: True friendship requires a choice between acceptance and hostility.
summaries.cot_proverb: "A single action can carry multiple, contradictory meanings."
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Misinterpretation of intent undermines genuine connection and understanding.

fable_text: A man saw a dog walking by and threw him some bits of food. The dog then said to the man, 'O man, keep away from me! All your well-wishing warns me to be even more on my guard.'
ground_truth_moral: People who offer to give someone many gifts are no doubt trying to deceive.

summaries.direct_moral: Excessive kindness can breed suspicion and danger.
summaries.cot_proverb: Beware the false kindness of those who seek to control or manipulate.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Unsolicited kindness can provoke suspicion and invite further harm.

fable_text: There was a man who had two dogs. He taught one to hunt and the other to be a watchdog. Whenever the hunting dog caught something, the watchdog would also share in the spoils. This made the hunting dog angry at the watchdog, since he had to work for everything he had while the watchdog lived off the fruits of his labour without doing anything. The watchdog retorted, 'Don't blame me! It's our master's fault. Since he didn't teach me how to work, I only know how to eat the food that others earn.'
ground_truth_moral: Do not blame others for the circumstances of their upbringing.

summaries.direct_moral: Dependence breeds resentment when one's labor is unacknowledged.
summaries.cot_proverb: The fruit of one's labor is often diminished when others benefit from it without contributing.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Cooperation without reciprocal contribution leads to resentment when one party perceives an unfair distribution of benefits.

fable_text: A pack of Wolves lurked near the Sheep pasture. But the Dogs kept them all at a respectful distance, and the Sheep grazed in perfect safety. But now the Wolves thought of a plan to trick the Sheep.'Why is there always this hostility between us?' they said. 'If it were not for those Dogs who are always stirring up trouble, I am sure we should get along beautifully. Send them away and you will see what good friends we shall become.' The Sheep were easily fooled. They persuaded the Dogs to go away, and that very evening the Wolves had the grandest feast of their lives.
ground_truth_moral: Do not give up friends for foes.

summaries.direct_moral: Unnecessary conflict can lead to unforeseen disaster.
summaries.cot_proverb: Moral: Do not trust those who cause trouble, for their removal may invite greater harm.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Unchecked antagonism invites greater peril for all involved.

fable_text: A tuna fish who was being chased by a dolphin was splashing madly through the water. Just when the dolphin was about to catch him, the tuna fish heaved himself forward with a great effort and landed on an island. Matching his effort, the dolphin ran aground beside him. The tuna fish then turned to look at the gasping dolphin and said, 'I do not grieve over my own death, so long as I am able to see that the one to blame is dying together with me!'
ground_truth_moral: There is satisfaction, however wretched, in taking one's enemy along if one has to die.

summaries.direct_moral: True solidarity is found in shared adversity.
summaries.cot_proverb: The strength of self-preservation can sometimes be overshadowed by a desire for shared misfortune.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Shared peril fosters a unified response to adversity.

fable_text: A donkey who had a sore on his back was grazing in a meadow. A raven alighted on his back and began to peck at the wound, while the donkey brayed and reared up on his hind legs in pain. The donkey's driver, meanwhile, stood off at a distance and laughed. A wolf who was passing by saw the whole thing and said to himself, 'How unfairly we wolves are treated! When people so much as catch a glimpse of us, they drive us away, but when someone like that raven makes his move, everyone just smiles at him.'
ground_truth_moral: Some can do wrong more easily than others can see it.

summaries.direct_moral: Unjust suffering is often met with indifference or mockery.
summaries.cot_proverb: When the weak are harmed, the strong are often indifferent to the suffering, and the vulnerable are further scorned.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Moral indifference to suffering is often masked by a lack of courage to intervene.

fable_text: The fox and the donkey were partners in a hunting expedition but when they encountered a lion, the fox recognized the danger they were in. She went to the lion and offered to betray the donkey if the lion would promise to spare her life in return. The lion agreed to let the fox go, and the fox then led the donkey into a trap and made him fall in. Once he saw that the donkey could not escape, the lion immediately seized the fox, saving the donkey for later.
ground_truth_moral: Traitors may expect treachery.

summaries.direct_moral: Self-interest often leads to betrayal, even when a seemingly advantageous deal is struck.
summaries.cot_proverb: When self-interest overrides loyalty, the clever will often turn against its allies.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Self-interest often leads to the betrayal of an ally for personal gain, resulting in the downfall of the self-interested party.

fable_text: A donkey put on the skin of a lion and went around frightening all the animals. The donkey saw a fox and tried to frighten her too, but she had heard his voice first, so she said to the donkey, 'You can be sure that I too would have been afraid, if I had not already heard the sound of your bray.'
ground_truth_moral: Clothes may disguise a fool, but his words will give him away.

summaries.direct_moral: Prejudice blinds one to the true nature of another's experience.
summaries.cot_proverb: The louder voice precedes the perceived threat.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The danger of imitation without originality is to be judged by the actions of others rather than one's own inherent nature.

fable_text: There was a man who kept a goat and a donkey. The goat was jealous of the donkey because he was given more to eat, so she made a deceptive proposal to the donkey, under the guise of giving him advice. 'Look,' said the goat, 'you are always being punished, constantly having to turn the millstone or carry burdens on your back. Why don't you pretend to have a seizure and throw yourself into a ditch?' The donkey trusted the goat and did what she told him to do. As a result of the fall, the donkey was badly scraped and bruised. The donkey's owner summoned a doctor to recommend a remedy. The doctor said that the donkey could be cured by a potion made from the lungs of a goat. So they slaughtered the unfortunate goat, who was thus trapped in her own snare while the donkey was saved.
ground_truth_moral: People who lay traps for others bring about their own destruction.

summaries.direct_moral: Jealousy and deceit often lead to self-destruction.
summaries.cot_proverb: The greater the disparity in power or circumstance, the more vulnerable the weaker party is to manipulation, and the manipulator often seeks to exploit the situation for their own gain, even at the cost of their own well-being.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Self-interest and malice lead to mutual destruction.

fable_text: A donkey and a horse belonged to the same man, and each of them did his duty. But the horse was granted many special privileges: he had plenty of food to eat, his flowing mane was braided and decorated, and his grooms washed him down with water each and every day. The donkey, on the other hand, was always bent down under the weight of the burdens he had to carry. Then one day the horse's owner mounted him and rode off into battle. In the clash of opposing forces, the horse was wounded on more than one occasion. When the donkey saw how the horse had been degraded, he congratulated himself on his hard-working life of labour.
ground_truth_moral: Be content with your station.

summaries.direct_moral: Excessive luxury can lead to a lack of appreciation for honest, diligent labor.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true measure of worth is not in superficial advantages but in the honest endurance of labor.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of superficial advantage and ease can lead to a misplaced sense of self-worth when contrasted with diligent, unrewarded labor.

fable_text: A horse who was overly proud of his elegant trappings happened to run into a donkey. The donkey was worn out with work and slow to make way for the approaching horse. 'I am tempted to smash you to pieces with my hooves,' said the horse. The donkey said nothing and only groaned, calling upon the gods to take note of his suffering. Not long afterwards, the horse, now a broken down wreck from his life on the race track, was sent to work on a farm. When the donkey saw the horse carrying a load of manure, he laughed and said, 'What has happened to you, who were once so proud of your elegant trappings? Time has reduced you to the wretchedness you formerly scorned!'
ground_truth_moral: Be cautious of pride, as nobody knows what the future may bring.

summaries.direct_moral: Pride leads to downfall, while humility finds a form of redemption.
summaries.cot_proverb: The arrogance of the proud leads to a downfall, while the suffering of the humble is overlooked until it is too late.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Pride leads to vulnerability, which is ultimately exposed by the harsh realities of time and circumstance.

fable_text: A donkey asked a horse to give him a nibble of barley. The horse replied, 'If only I could, I would do so gladly! Indeed, I would give you a great deal of barley, since I am a noble and generous character. When we return to the stable together this evening, I promise to give you an entire sack of grain.' The donkey then said to the horse, 'If you refuse to do me even this small favour now, why should I expect a still bigger favour in the future?'
ground_truth_moral: People are quick to promise but reluctant to give.

summaries.direct_moral: A promise of future favor is only as valuable as the present deed.
summaries.cot_proverb: If you seek a favor, be prepared to offer a greater one in return.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Conditional promises and perceived generosity can mask insincerity or manipulative intent.

fable_text: A lion chose a donkey as his hunting companion and hid him in the bushes, ordering the donkey to frighten the wild animals with his unfamiliar voice while the lion ambushed the fleeing animals. Following the lion's instructions, our long-eared friend immediately began to bray with all his might. The animals were startled by this strange and amazing sound and they ran in terror toward their familiar hiding places, thus falling victim to the lion's violent attack. When the lion was exhausted by the slaughter, he summoned the donkey and told him to be quiet. The insolent creature then said to the lion, 'And what did you think of my vocalizing efforts?' 'Truly remarkable,' said the lion. 'In fact, if I didn't know already that you were a donkey born and bred, I also would have fled in fear.'
ground_truth_moral: The loud-mouthed boaster does not impress nor frighten those who know him.

summaries.direct_moral: Blind obedience to a superior can lead to self-destruction.
summaries.cot_proverb: The more cunning or powerful entity may attempt to manipulate a weaker one into performing a task, the weaker one will ultimately expose the manipulator's own vulnerability.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Trusting external guidance without understanding the underlying context leads to self-destructive actions.

fable_text: A donkey saw a lyre lying in a field. He approached the instrument and as he tried to strum it with his hoof, the strings resounded at his touch. 'What a beautiful thing,' said the donkey, 'but completely inappropriate, since I don't know anything about music. If only someone better equipped than myself had found it, my ears would have been delighted by heavenly melodies!'
ground_truth_moral: Talent is wasted when it's in the hands of the unappreciative.

summaries.direct_moral: Inadequacy prevents appreciation of beauty.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true value of something is often missed by those who lack the proper skill or context to appreciate it.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The limitation of one's experience prevents appreciation of something beyond one's current capacity.

fable_text: A donkey and a mule were walking along together. When the donkey saw that they were carrying equal loads, he got angry and complained that the mule was awarded a double portion of food even though she carried a load that was no bigger than his own. After they had journeyed a little further down the road, the driver saw that the donkey could not hold up under the weight, so he took part of the donkey's load and placed it on the mule. Later, when the driver saw that the donkey had grown even more tired, he again transferred some of the donkey's load to the mule, and so on. Finally the driver took the entire load and shifted it from the donkey to the mule. At that point the mule glanced over at the donkey and said, 'What do you say now: don't I deserve a double portion of food?'
ground_truth_moral: Instead of looking at how things start, we should look instead at how they turn out in the end.

summaries.direct_moral: Unequal burdens lead to mutual resentment when fairness is questioned.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true measure of worth is not in the quantity of what is carried, but in the fairness of the distribution.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of personal grievance leads to a cycle of mutual exploitation, ultimately revealing that self-centeredness undermines any claim to fairness.

fable_text: There was a donkey who worked for a gardener. Because the gardener made the donkey work very hard but gave him very little food, the donkey prayed to Zeus to take him away from the gardener and give him to another master, so Zeus sent Hermes to sell the donkey to a potter. The donkey also found this situation unbearable, since he was forced to carry even heavier loads than before. He called upon Zeus again, and this time Zeus arranged for the donkey to be purchased by a tanner. When the donkey saw the kind of work the tanner did, he said, 'Oh, it would have been better for me to have kept on working for my previous masters in a state of starvation! Now I have ended up in a place where I won't even get a proper burial after I die.'
ground_truth_moral: People often appreciate what they had only after experiencing something worse.

summaries.direct_moral: Desperation can lead to misguided desires that ultimately prove as perilous as the original hardship.
summaries.cot_proverb: Moral: A desire for relief from hardship often leads to new forms of distress.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Desperation can lead to a misguided desire for change that ultimately fails to address the root cause of suffering.

fable_text: An onager saw a donkey labouring under a heavy load and he made fun of the donkey's enslavement. 'Lucky me!' said the onager. 'I am free from bondage and do not have to work for anyone else, since I have grass near at hand on the hillsides, while you rely on someone else to feed you, forever oppressed by slavery and its blows!' At that very moment a lion happened to appear on the scene. He did not come near the donkey since the donkey's driver was standing beside him. The onager, however, was all alone, so the lion attacked and devoured him.
ground_truth_moral: The insubordinate are free from both obligation and protection.

summaries.direct_moral: Pride in one's freedom invites ruin when true danger appears.
summaries.cot_proverb: The envious person mocks another's hardship, only to suffer the same fate when misfortune strikes.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Self-serving criticism leads to unforeseen peril.

fable_text: A cowardly old man had led his donkey out to pasture. At the unexpected sound of the enemy approaching, the old man was stricken with terror and tried to persuade the donkey to run away so that he wouldn't be captured. The donkey obstinately asked the old man, 'Tell me, do you suppose the victor will make me carry two pack saddles instead of one?' The old man said he did not think so. 'I rest my case,' concluded the donkey. 'What difference does it make who my master is, if I always carry one saddle at a time?'
ground_truth_moral: In a change of government, the poor change nothing beyond the name of their master.

summaries.direct_moral: Fearful reliance on others' capabilities is misplaced when facing true peril.
summaries.cot_proverb: Moral: Fear of the master blinds one to the true nature of the peril.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Fearful reliance on others' perceived competence leads to a failure to recognize the fundamental limitations of the situation.

fable_text: There was a man who wanted to buy a donkey. He selected one donkey for further examination and led him to where his own donkeys were, leaving him by the feeding trough. The new donkey went and stood next to the donkey who was the laziest and greediest of them all, ignoring all the others. As the new donkey showed no signs of any better behaviour, the man led him away again, returning him to his former master. When asked whether he had given the donkey a fair chance, the man explained, 'I don't even need to put him to the test: I know that he will be just the same as the one he chose for his companion.'
ground_truth_moral: A man is known by the company he keeps.

summaries.direct_moral: True comparison reveals inherent character.
summaries.cot_proverb: "Do not test a creature against its own kind."
summaries.conceptual_abstract: True merit is revealed by the inherent character of the individual, regardless of external testing.

fable_text: There was a man who had vowed that he would sacrifice a pig in honour of Hercules if the god agreed to rescue him from danger. When the man fulfilled his vow and sacrificed the pig, he then ordered that the pig's leftover barley be given to the donkey. The donkey, however, refused to touch it. 'This is the kind of food that would normally arouse my appetite,' said the horse, 'but not when it is the result of the previous diner having had his throat cut!'
ground_truth_moral: Easy life and seeming good fortune of others conceal a threat to their welfare.

summaries.direct_moral: A vow fulfilled without consideration can lead to unintended consequences.
summaries.cot_proverb: The cost of a vow is the inability to benefit from the consequences of that vow.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The consequence of a vow is the creation of an obligation that is then selectively enforced or misinterpreted by others based on the context of the preceding action.

fable_text: The donkeys were tired of being burdened with burdens and labouring all the days of their lives, so they sent ambassadors to Zeus, asking him to release them from their toil. Zeus, wanting to show them that they had asked for something impossible, said that their suffering would come to an end on the day when they pissed a river. The donkeys took him seriously and to this day whenever donkeys see where another donkey has pissed, they come to a halt and piss in the same place.
ground_truth_moral: One cannot circumvent his destiny.

summaries.direct_moral: Unrealistic demands invite absurd or impossible consequences.
summaries.cot_proverb: The depth of one's suffering is often met with an equally absurd or impossible demand, leading to a bizarre and literal fulfillment of the request.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The limits of expectation are often met with absurd or disproportionate responses, leading to a cycle where a desperate plea is answered with a condition that is both impossible and ultimately self-defeating, resulting in a bizarre, literal enactment of the very thing they sought to escape.

fable_text: A donkey and a rooster lived together on a farm. A lion who had noticed the donkey crept up and was about to pounce when the rooster let loose a squawk. This frightened the lion (for they say that lions are terrified of the rooster's crowing) and he turned tail and ran. The donkey was elated at the thought of the lion running away from a rooster. He took off in pursuit of the lion but when the donkey had gone some distance away from the farm, the lion turned around and ate him.
ground_truth_moral: False confidence often leads into danger.

summaries.direct_moral: Distraction can lead to unforeseen peril.
summaries.cot_proverb: The trick of the crow can save one from a greater peril, but misplaced excitement can lead to a more foolish fate.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Fear can be misplaced, leading to misguided pursuit and ultimate peril.

fable_text: A donkey had turned aside from the main road and was heading for a cliff. The driver shouted at him, 'Where are you going, you wretched beast?' He grabbed hold of the donkey's tail and tried to drag him back from the cliff, but the donkey did not stop and instead kept going forward. So the man pushed the donkey even harder than he had pulled him back and said, 'Go ahead then! You can take the worthless victor's crown in this damned contest.'
ground_truth_moral: You can't always stop a fool.

summaries.direct_moral: Persistence in pursuing a goal justifies the pursuit even when it seems perilous.
summaries.cot_proverb: Moral: Even when pulled back, some paths are inevitable.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Courage in the face of overwhelming danger is often misplaced when confronted with the inherent, unyielding nature of a determined pursuit.

fable_text: A jackdaw saw that the doves in a dovecote were very well fed, so he dyed himself white and went to join them, expecting to share in their food. So long as the jackdaw kept quiet, the doves thought he was another dove and accepted him, but when he forgot to keep quiet and let out a squawk, the pigeons then recognized who he was and they pecked at him until he went away. Unable to feed with the doves, he then went back again to the jackdaws. But because they did not recognize his colour, they kept him away from their food and the jackdaw ended up with nothing to eat at all.
ground_truth_moral: Greed for more can lead to losing what you already have.

summaries.direct_moral: Appearance can deceive, and a lack of true identity leads to isolation and want.
summaries.cot_proverb: The quieter creature is often mistaken for a friend until their true nature is revealed, leading to isolation and loss.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Appearance can deceive, but true belonging requires authentic selfhood.

fable_text: While excavating her den, a fox dug a hole in the earth and as she made deeper and deeper tunnels in the ground, she finally reached the cave of a dragon who was guarding a hidden treasure. When the fox saw the dragon, she said, 'First of all, I beg your pardon for this carelessness on my part; second, you no doubt realize that gold means nothing to me, so I hope that you will be so kind as to explain to me what profit you gain from this work, and what reward could be so great that you would forgo the pleasure of sleep and live out your life here in the dark?' 'I have no reward,' the dragon replied, 'but this task was assigned to me by Jupiter on high.' 'Does that mean you take nothing for yourself and do not give anything to anyone?' 'That is what the Fates have decreed.' 'Please don't be angry then if I speak freely,' concluded the fox, 'but someone who lives like this must have been born under an unlucky star!'
ground_truth_moral: A life of greed and hoarding wealth leads to misery.

summaries.direct_moral: Greed blinds one to the true value of things, even when faced with immense power.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true measure of a person is not in what they possess, but in the wisdom of their experience and the humility to seek understanding beyond superficial gain.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Self-interest often obscures the true value of transcendent purpose.

fable_text: Some men were making their way along the beach and reached a lookout point. From there, they could see a piece of driftwood floating far off in the distance, and they thought that it must be an enormous ship. They waited for a long time, thinking that the ship would put in to shore nearby. As the driftwood was blown closer to shore by the wind, they kept on waiting, but they no longer thought it was a great ship as before; they were now expecting some kind of smaller boat. As it was carried in even closer, they saw that it was just a piece of driftwood. The men then said to one another, 'How foolish of us! We had high expectations for something that was actually nothing.'
ground_truth_moral: Our mere anticipations of life outrun its realities.

summaries.direct_moral: High expectations for something that is actually nothing leads to folly.
summaries.cot_proverb: The expectation of grandeur blinds one to the reality of simple things.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Unchecked desire blinds perception to reality.

fable_text: There was a woman whose husband was always drunk, so she came up with a plan to cure him of his drinking problem. After he had passed out one night and was sleeping the sleep of the dead, she picked him up and carried him on her shoulders to the common cemetery. Then she put him down on the ground and left him there. She waited until he had time to sober up, then she went and knocked at the entrance to the cemetery. Her husband shouted, 'Who's there?' She answered, 'I am the one who brings food to the dead.' Her husband shouted back, 'I don't want anything to eat, but bring me something to drink, my good man! It pains me to hear you speaking of food but saying nothing about a drink!' The woman then beat her breast and exclaimed, 'Woe is me! My ingenuity has not accomplished anything! O my husband, you have not simply failed to learn your lesson: you are actually even worse than before. Your problem has turned out to be permanent!'
ground_truth_moral: It's hard to give up bad habits.

summaries.direct_moral: Ingenuity alone cannot solve a deeply ingrained problem, and sometimes a misguided attempt can worsen the situation.
summaries.cot_proverb: "Ingenuity misplaced is worse than ignorance."
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The attempt to solve a problem through unconventional or misguided means ultimately fails to address the root cause and can exacerbate the original issue.

fable_text: There were once some birds who gathered together for a group assembly and debated amongst themselves who was best suited to rule. The peacock said to the other birds, 'The kingship suits me best, since I am remarkably beautiful and in the prime of life.' While the rest of the birds were satisfied with the peacock, the raven made his way into their midst and protested, 'Tell me, if you become king, what is going to happen when the eagle attacks us: are you strong enough to rescue us from his assault?'
ground_truth_moral: Beauty alone does not qualify one for leadership.

summaries.direct_moral: Beauty does not guarantee strength or true leadership.
summaries.cot_proverb: Moral: A person's suitability for a position is less important than their ability to face actual danger.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Self-proclaimed superiority blinds one to necessary collective strength and diverse capabilities.

fable_text: The hares were at war with an eagle and they asked the foxes to be their allies. The foxes said in reply, 'We would agree to be your allies, if we didn't know what sort of creatures you are and who you are fighting against!'
ground_truth_moral: Count the cost before you commit yourselves.

summaries.direct_moral: True alliance requires mutual understanding and shared opposition.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true measure of an alliance is mutual understanding and shared purpose, not blind agreement.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Contextual understanding is necessary for genuine cooperation.

fable_text: Two roosters were fighting with one another. The loser hid himself away in a corner, while the rooster who had won the battle flew up on top of the house and flapped his wings, crowing about his victory. An eagle then swooped down and carried the rooster away.
ground_truth_moral: Pride goes before destruction.

summaries.direct_moral: Hubris invites a greater downfall.
summaries.cot_proverb: The greater the boast, the greater the fall.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of singular glory often invites further, more destructive forms of predation or loss.

fable_text: An eagle and a Fox formed an intimate friendship and decided to live near each other. The Eagle built her nest in the branches of a tall tree, while the Fox crept into the underwood and there produced her young. Not long after they had agreed upon this plan, the Eagle, being in want of provision for her young ones, swooped down while the Fox was out, seized upon one of the little cubs, and feasted herself and her brood. The Fox on her return, discovered what had happened, but was less grieved for the death of her young than for her inability to avenge them. A just retribution, however, quickly fell upon the Eagle. While hovering near an altar, on which some villagers were sacrificing a goat, she suddenly seized a piece of the flesh, and carried it, along with a burning cinder, to her nest. A strong breeze soon fanned the spark into a flame, and the eaglets, as yet unfledged and helpless, were roasted in their nest and dropped down dead at the bottom of the tree. There, in the sight of the Eagle, the Fox gobbled them up.
ground_truth_moral: God is the ultimate judge.

summaries.direct_moral: Greed and betrayal will ultimately lead to the downfall of the betrayer.
summaries.cot_proverb: The serpent that strikes first is often undone by the very shadow it casts.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of self-interest, even when cloaked in apparent cooperation, inevitably leads to the destruction of the vulnerable, and the subsequent pursuit of vengeance only compounds the initial injustice.

fable_text: A hare had been seized by an eagle and was weeping bitter tears. Meanwhile, a sparrow was making fun of the hare and said, 'So, what became of your fabled swiftness? How did your feet happen to fail you?' While the sparrow was still speaking, he was caught off guard by a hawk who killed the sparrow as he was still shrieking his useless cries of protest. The hare, by now no more than half-alive, remarked, 'Ah, this makes my dying easier: a moment ago you were making fun of my misfortune, confident in your own safety, but now you are bewailing your fate with a lament that matches my own.'
ground_truth_moral: It is a foolish thing to give advice to others while not looking out for oneself.

summaries.direct_moral: Mockery of misfortune often leads to a more severe downfall.
summaries.cot_proverb: The mirror of folly is the downfall of the fool.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Mutual misfortune often mirrors the inherent fragility of life.

fable_text: An eagle was sitting sadly in her tree when she was joined by a kite. The kite said to the eagle, 'Why do I see you looking so sad?' The eagle replied, 'I cannot help but be sad at heart, since I need a mate who is my equal but I cannot find one anywhere.' The kite then said to the eagle, 'You should marry me! I am even stronger than you are, which makes me the ideal bird for you.' The eagle asked him, 'Just what kinds of prey are you able to catch?' The kite said, 'Well, on several occasions I have managed to capture an ostrich in my talons and eat it.' When she heard this, the eagle accepted the kite's proposal and married him. After the wedding ceremony and festivities were finished, the eagle said to the kite, 'Go and get us some of that prey which you promised.' The high-flying kite was only able to offer the eagle a nasty little mouse whose flesh was all putrid and rotten. The eagle said, 'Is this what you promised?' The kite replied, 'In order to make this most eminent match with you, I had no choice but to agree to anything you asked, even if it meant promising something impossible.'
ground_truth_moral: Some people will do anything to get what they want.

summaries.direct_moral: True partnership is often based on insincere promises and unequal capabilities.
summaries.cot_proverb: The willingness to compromise one's integrity for a desired relationship or gain is often met with a reciprocal, yet ultimately disappointing, fulfillment of the promise.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of an idealized partnership leads to a compromise of genuine needs and trust.

fable_text: An earthworm saw a snake stretched out and envied his length. The earthworm wanted to be as long as that snake, so he lay down beside the snake and tried to extend himself. The worm stretched and stretched until he accidentally split into pieces.
ground_truth_moral: He who competes with his superiors destroys himself before he can equal them.

summaries.direct_moral: Excessive envy of another's attributes leads to self-destruction.
summaries.cot_proverb: The moral is: Ambition for others' stature invites ruin.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Hubris leads to self-destruction.

fable_text: There were two enemies who had set sail on the same ship. They wanted to keep their distance from one another, so one went up on the prow while the other took the stern. Each man then stayed where he was. Soon a fierce storm began to blow and the ship foundered. The man standing at the stern asked the helmsman which part of the ship was likely to sink first. 'It will be the prow,' said the helmsman. 'So be it!' said the man. 'The thought of death does not trouble me so long as I will see my enemy die before me!'
ground_truth_moral: Some people are so consumed by hatred that they would rather suffer themselves than see their enemies succeed.

summaries.direct_moral: The desire to see an enemy's downfall blinds one to the true peril.
summaries.cot_proverb: Moral: Vengeance blinds one to greater danger.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of personal advantage in a shared peril leads to a destructive focus on others' downfall rather than a commitment to shared survival.

fable_text: A plowman loosed his oxen from the yoke and led them away to be watered. Meanwhile, a hungry wolf, who was looking for something to eat, discovered the plow and started to lick the yoke straps where the oxen had been tied. The unsuspecting wolf slowly but surely slipped his neck beneath the yoke, until he was not able to pull it back out. He then started dragging the plow along the furrow. When the plowman came back and saw what had happened, he said, 'O you wicked creature, if only you would give up your life of theft and crime in order to devote yourself entirely to farming!'
ground_truth_moral: People with a history of wrongdoing are often not trusted.

summaries.direct_moral: Greed for what is not earned will ultimately lead to ruin.
summaries.cot_proverb: The moral of the story is that a creature focused on illicit gain will ultimately destroy itself through its own misguided pursuit.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Self-interest often leads to unforeseen and destructive consequences for others.

fable_text: There was once a race-horse who had grown old and was sold to grind in the mill. Harnessed to the mill-stone, he ground grain all day long and into in the evening. As he was working, the old horse groaned aloud and said, 'Once I ran in the races, but now I must run in circles around this millers' course!'
ground_truth_moral: Do not boast too much at the height of your powers; people often spend their old age worn out with toil and trouble.

summaries.direct_moral: Age brings a realization that past glories cannot be reclaimed.
summaries.cot_proverb: The old cannot change the nature of their toil.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The passage illustrates the futility of clinging to past achievements when facing unavoidable decline.

fable_text: A eunuch was involved in a legal dispute with an extremely unpleasant person who made rude and cutting remarks, and even insulted the eunuch for the loss he had suffered in his bodily parts. The eunuch responded: 'I admit that this is the one thing that puts me in considerable difficulty: I don't have testi-mony, so to speak, in support of my good character. But you are a fool to denounce me for something that is merely a matter of fate!'
ground_truth_moral: The only thing that can really bring shame on a man is a punishment that he has justly deserved.

summaries.direct_moral: A person should not judge another based on misfortune without considering the full context.
summaries.cot_proverb: Moral: The wound of fate is not a sufficient weapon for the fool.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The defense of one's inherent worth against baseless attacks on personal suffering.

fable_text: One day the kite happened to consider his wings and feet and talons. 'Indeed,' he exclaimed, 'Am I not just as well armed as the hawk and the falcon? Look at what wings and what feet and what talons I have! Why shouldn't I go catch some partridges?' The kite knew a place where he could find many partridges so he went there and launched his attack: he seized one partridge with his beak, another with his wings, and one more in each foot. But the kite couldn't keep hold of that many partridges, so in the end he had none. Hence the saying: Seize all, lose all. From then on, the kite never tried to hunt wild birds again.
ground_truth_moral: Do not try to grasp too much at once.

summaries.direct_moral: Greed in attempting to grasp too much leads to the loss of everything.
summaries.cot_proverb: Seize all, lose all.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Greed in pursuing too many opportunities leads to the loss of all.

fable_text: A stag had been hiding in the woods when he was discovered by some hunters. Hoping to escape certain death at their hands, he ran blindly in terror towards the nearest farmhouse and concealed himself in a convenient stall where the oxen were kept. One of the oxen said to the fugitive, 'You wretched creature, what on earth are you trying to do? You have sealed your own death warrant by trusting your life to the protection of a human house!' But the stag implored the oxen, 'Have mercy, I beg you! At the first opportunity, I'll run back out again.' The passing hours of the day gave way to night. A cowherd brought some leafy boughs into the stall but saw nothing amiss. The various farm workers came and went, but no one noticed a thing; the bailiff also passed through but even he didn't observe anything out of the ordinary. The stag was delighted and began thanking the oxen who had kept quiet on his behalf and had extended such welcome hospitality in a moment of need. One of the oxen said to the stag, 'We do indeed wish you all the best, but if the man of a hundred eyes should come, your life will hang in the balance.' Meanwhile, after dinner, the master himself came to inspect the manger since he had noticed that the oxen had been looking rather sickly. 'Why is there so little fodder here?' he shouted. 'And look, not enough bedding! And how much trouble would it be to get rid of these spider webs?' As the master examined each and every thing, he also noticed the stag's tall horns. He called his servants and ordered them to kill the stag and to carry his carcass away.
ground_truth_moral: Nothing escapes the master's eye.

summaries.direct_moral: Blind trust in temporary help invites greater peril.
summaries.cot_proverb: Trusting others in a moment of desperation can lead to unforeseen and more severe consequences.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Blind trust in temporary aid can lead to unforeseen and more severe consequences.

fable_text: A wicked farmer envied his neighbour's abundant crops. In order to destroy the fruits of that man's labour, he caught a fox, attached a blazing fire-brand to her tail and then let the fox loose in his neighbour's crops. The fox, however, did not go where she was sent. Instead, as fate decreed, she set fire to the crops of the man who had let her loose.
ground_truth_moral: Bad neighbours are the first to suffer from the harm they would do to others.

summaries.direct_moral: Envy and malice often bring about unforeseen and destructive consequences for the perpetrator.
summaries.cot_proverb: Moral: The trouble you cause often finds its way back to you.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Moral principle: Malice often breeds unforeseen and self-destructive consequences.

fable_text: A lion entered a farmer's yard and the farmer, wanting to capture the lion, shut the outer gate. The lion, unable to get out, first devoured all the sheep and then turned his attention to the cattle. The farmer became afraid for his own safety so he opened the door. After the lion had gone away, the farmer's wife saw the farmer groaning and said to him, 'It serves you right! Why did you want to shut yourself up with the sort of creature you should run away from even at a distance?'
ground_truth_moral: Do not take on more than you can handle.

summaries.direct_moral: Fearful attempts to control a dangerous situation often invite greater peril.
summaries.cot_proverb: The attempt to control a powerful force often backfires, and excessive caution or misguided action can lead to unintended consequences and subsequent criticism.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Fearful attempts to control overwhelming threats often lead to unintended consequences that harm the self.

fable_text: There were once some wasps and some partridges who were terribly thirsty, so they went to a farmer to ask him for a drink. In return for the water, the partridges promised that they would dig around his vines so that they would produce excellent grapes, while the wasps would stand guard over the vines, driving away thieves by stinging them. The farmer said to them, 'But look, I have these two oxen, who do everything for me without making bargains. It is better for me to give the water to them, not to you.'
ground_truth_moral: Charity begins at home, but this does not prevent the further exercise of it.

summaries.direct_moral: Trustworthy commitments are more valuable than deceptive promises.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true value of selfless dedication outweighs the promise of conditional gain.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Trust in unearned, inherent capability is superior to manipulative promises.

fable_text: A certain farmer was using his cattle to haul manure out of the stables. The cattle complained to the farmer that their labour allowed him to harvest his wheat and barley crops, supplying his household with ample food year in and year out. Therefore, said the cattle, it was hardly fair for them to have to perform the vile task of hauling manure out of the stables. The farmer then asked, 'Is it not the case that you yourselves are the source of the substance which you are now carrying away?' The cattle replied, 'Yes, that is true.' The farmer then concluded, 'So, since you are the ones who made a mess of the stable in your spare time, it is only right that you should also make some effort to clean it up!'
ground_truth_moral: Those who complain about their duties often forget their own role in creating the situation.

summaries.direct_moral: Those who benefit from another's labor should also contribute to its upkeep.
summaries.cot_proverb: The moral is that those who benefit from a situation should also take responsibility for its upkeep.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The exploitation of others' labor for one's own benefit, even when framed as a necessity, can lead to a reciprocal demand for fairness.

fable_text: In the house of a certain farmer there lived a snake who regularly came to the table and was fed on scraps of food. Not long afterwards the farmer grew rich, but then he became angry at the snake and tried to attack him with an axe. The farmer then lost his wealth and he realized that he had prospered because of the good luck he had gained from the snake before having wounded him. The farmer then begged the snake to forgive him for his evil deed, and the snake replied, 'You are sorry for what you have done, but you must not expect me to be your faithful friend until this scar heals. It is not possible for me to be truly reconciled to you until all thought of that treacherous axe has left my mind.'
ground_truth_moral: He who harms someone should henceforth be treated with suspicion.

summaries.direct_moral: Do not punish a source of your fortune until your own misguided actions have healed.
summaries.cot_proverb: The wound of past exploitation prevents true reconciliation.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Unjustified retribution destroys the very source of one's fortune.

fable_text: There was a snake who used to lurk around the front door of a farmer's house. One day the snake struck the man's son, biting him on the foot. The boy died on the spot. The boy's parents were filled with immense sorrow and the grief-stricken father seized his axe and tried to kill the malevolent snake. When the snake fled his pursuer, the man hurried after him, raising his weapon, determined to strike, but as the farmer was about to deal the snake a deadly blow, he missed and managed only to cut off the tip of his tail. The man was terrified at the thought that he might have killed the snake, so he took cakes and water along with honey and salt and called to the snake, wanting to make peace with him. The snake, however, only hissed softly at the farmer from where he had hidden himself in the rocks and said: 'Man, do not trouble yourself any longer: there can be no possible friendship between us any more. When I look upon my tail, I am in pain. The same is true for you: whenever you look again upon the grave of your son, you will not be able to live in peace with me.'
ground_truth_moral: Injuries may be forgiven, but not forgotten.

summaries.direct_moral: Vengeance for a past wrong only breeds further sorrow.
summaries.cot_proverb: When the heart is consumed by past suffering, it cannot find solace or reconciliation.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of vengeance and the desire for reconciliation both fail to resolve the underlying suffering, demonstrating that past harm cannot be erased by present actions or attempts at peace.

fable_text: Two wolves crossed paths one day and began to talk about the fact that they were an object of universal hatred. People would even start yelling at them when they had no intention of doing anything wrong. One of the wolves then said to the other, 'It's all because they've never seen us do anything good! If the people saw us do them a favour even just once, then they would be more inclined to expect us to be good later on.' The second wolf asked, 'What good thing do you think we can do that will convince the people of our good behaviour?' The first wolf replied, 'Let's leave the woods and go into the fields where we can help the people gathering sheaves!' So the wolves came out of the woods and went into the fields and gathered the sheaves, just as they had planned. But as soon as the people saw the wolves in the field, they immediately chased them away, shouting and screaming. The wolves were surprised and said, 'What do they mean by shouting at us like that, when we are not doing any harm and only trying to help them?' The other wolf replied, 'Let's go back home and act as we did in the past. Whether we help the people or harass them, they are going to hate us just the same!'
ground_truth_moral: When wicked people do not get the reward and thanks they expect, they immediately put a stop to their good works.

summaries.direct_moral: Showing good deeds is often ineffective against ingrained prejudice.
summaries.cot_proverb: The past cannot be erased by a single good deed.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The perception of others is often shaped by past experiences and the expectation of future behavior, regardless of current actions.

fable_text: A rich old farmer, who felt that he had not many more days to live, called his sons to his bedside. 'My sons,' he said, 'heed what I have to say to you. Do not on any account part with the estate that has belonged to our family for so many generations. Somewhere on it is hidden a rich treasure. I do not know the exact spot, but it is there, and you will surely find it. Spare no energy and leave no spot unturned in your search.' The father died, and no sooner was he in his grave than the sons set to work digging with all their might, turning up every foot of ground with their spades, and going over the whole farm two or three times. No hidden gold did they find; but at harvest time when they had settled their accounts and had pocketed a rich profit far greater than that of any of their neighbors, they understood that the treasure their father had told them about was the wealth of a bountiful crop.
ground_truth_moral: Man's greatest treasure consists in work.

summaries.direct_moral: Blind faith in a hidden treasure often leads to the discovery of true, readily available abundance.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true treasure is often found in the diligent pursuit of what is readily available, rather than in blind faith in elusive promises.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of an illusory, hidden reward distracts from the recognition of tangible, present value.

fable_text: There was a timid old man who had an only child, a son, who was generally high-spirited and who wanted to go hunting. In a dream the father saw his son lying dead, killed by a lion. Afraid that this might really happen and that the dream might actually come true, he built a house for the men of the family, and it was an extremely beautiful house, with high ceilings and sturdy walls and full of sunlight. He then enclosed his son inside this house, locked away under guard. To keep his son from becoming sad, he had the walls decorated with pictures of various animals, and among all these animals there was a painted lion. Staring at the lion, the boy felt even more sad, and he eventually approached the lion and said, 'O you wicked animal, because you showed that lying dream to my father's eyes you are able to keep me prisoner here, watched by guards as if I were a woman. But why do I attack you only with words, and not with an act of violence as well?' The boy then dashed his hands against the lion, intending to scratch its eyes out, but instead a sliver of wood came off and stabbed him under his fingernail. This soon brought about a burning inflammation of the flesh, and although the desperate father did everything he could, it was all to no avail. The infection spread until it reached the boy's groin, and thus brought his life to an end. The old man was unable to save his child, who had been destined to die because of a lion who was not even alive.
ground_truth_moral: We had better bear our troubles bravely than try to escape them.

summaries.direct_moral: Fear of the unreal can lead to misguided actions that ultimately cause destruction.
summaries.cot_proverb: Beware the attempt to control or punish a perceived threat, for the very act of trying to subdue it can lead to unforeseen and devastating consequences.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Fear of potential harm can create self-fulfilling and destructive constraints.

fable_text: A fisherman was pulling in the net which he had just cast and, as luck would have it, the net was filled with all kinds of sea creatures. The little fish escaped to the bottom of the net and slipped out through its many holes, but the big fish was caught and lay stretched out flat aboard the boat.
ground_truth_moral: To be small is a way to stay safe and avoid problems, whereas you rarely see a man with a big reputation who is able to keep out of danger.

summaries.direct_moral: Hubris invites disaster, while caution can offer salvation.
summaries.cot_proverb: The greater fortune is often found in the apparent abundance, while the smaller, more elusive opportunities slip through unnoticed.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The inherent unpredictability of fortune dictates that even when a situation appears successful, unforeseen vulnerabilities can lead to loss, while greater, more substantial elements may still be secured.

fable_text: A fisherman was catching fish by the sea. A monkey saw him, and wanted to imitate what he was doing. The man went away into a little cave to take a rest, leaving his net on the beach. The monkey came and grabbed the net, thinking that he too would go fishing. But since he didn't know anything about it and had not had any training, the monkey got tangled up in the net, fell into the sea, and was drowned. The fisherman seized the monkey when he was already done for and said, 'You wretched creature! Your lack of judgment and stupid behaviour has cost you your life!'
ground_truth_moral: Imitation is no substitute for knowledge.

summaries.direct_moral: Lack of knowledge and poor judgment can lead to ruin.
summaries.cot_proverb: The lack of knowledge and experience in imitation leads to disastrous consequences for the inexperienced.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Unskilled imitation without understanding leads to ruin.

fable_text: Some fishermen were hauling in their net. It was quite heavy, so the fishermen made merry and danced for joy, thinking that they had landed a very big catch. Yet when they finally dragged it in, they found that the net contained only a few fish, together with a very large stone. The fishermen now grew extremely despondent, not so much because of the lack of fish but because they had been expecting just the opposite. Then one of the fishermen, an experienced old man, remarked, 'Let's not take it too hard, my friends! Given that grief seems to be the sister of joy, then we must expect to put up with some suffering precisely because we were so elated at first.'
ground_truth_moral: We have to endure reversals of fortune, since we know that life is a matter of luck.

summaries.direct_moral: Excessive elation often leads to disappointment when reality proves meager.
summaries.cot_proverb: Moral: Expectation breeds sorrow.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Anticipation of pleasure often leads to disproportionate suffering when reality fails to meet inflated expectations.

fable_text: Some fishermen had gone out fishing, and when they had struggled for a long time but had not managed to catch anything, they became very downcast and prepared to turn back. All of a sudden a tuna fish who was being chased by some bigger fish leaped into their boat. The men seized the tuna fish and went home rejoicing.
ground_truth_moral: Luck often bestows the things that skill cannot obtain.

summaries.direct_moral: Perseverance can lead to unexpected rewards.
summaries.cot_proverb: The depth of despair can be overcome by unexpected fortune.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Unexpected fortune can transform despair into joy.

fable_text: A fisherman was fishing in a river. He stretched out his nets and covered the river's stream from one side to the other. He then tied a stone to a piece of rope and struck the water with it so that the fish would flee and fall unwittingly into the net. Someone who lived in that neighbourhood saw what the man was doing and began to complain, because by agitating the water in this way he deprived them of clear water to drink. The fisherman answered, 'But if I do not disturb the river, I will have no choice but to die of hunger!'
ground_truth_moral: Some never understand the actions of others.

summaries.direct_moral: Necessity can justify actions that cause harm if survival is at stake.
summaries.cot_proverb: The need for sustenance versus the right to a peaceful existence.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Necessity can justify actions that cause harm when survival is at stake.

fable_text: A flea once asked an ox, 'Given that you are so exceedingly large and strong, why do you suffer this enslavement to people day after day? Look at me: I lacerate their flesh without pity, drinking great gulps of their blood!' The ox said, 'I cannot help but be grateful to the human race, since I am cherished and loved by them to an extraordinary degree, and since they often rub my forehead and shoulders.' The flea admitted, 'Woe is me! This rubbing of which you are so fond is the worst thing that can happen to me: when they do that, I die.'
ground_truth_moral: A reward for one might be destruction for another.

summaries.direct_moral: Excessive affection can lead to destruction.
summaries.cot_proverb: The greater the power, the more vulnerable the lesser being is to the attention of the powerful, and the greater the vulnerability of the powerful to the attention of the weak.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Dependence on external validation can blind one to the inherent suffering or exploitation of others.

fable_text: A man finally caught a flea that had been bothering him terribly. He shouted at the flea, 'Just who do you think you are, feeding on all the limbs of my body here and there, eating me up as you please?' The flea responded, 'That is how we live! Please don't kill me; I cannot be causing you too much harm.' The man laughed at the flea and said, 'I'm going to kill you here and now with my very own hands: any kind of evil, whether it is big or small, should not be allowed to exist under any circumstances whatsoever!'
ground_truth_moral: No mercy should be shown to someone who is wicked, regardless of whether his wickedness is great or small.

summaries.direct_moral: Even small evils must be dealt with decisively.
summaries.cot_proverb: The attempt to destroy a small evil with excessive force often results in the destruction of the self.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The assertion that all harm must be eliminated, regardless of the source or context, demonstrates a flawed approach to dealing with conflict and the inherent nature of existence.

fable_text: When the honey had been spilled in the pantry, some flies flew up and landed on it. Their feet got stuck and they could not fly away. As they were drowning in the honey, the flies remarked, 'Oh, woe is us! For the sake of a brief banquet, we are going to die!'
ground_truth_moral: Pleasure bought with pains, hurts.

summaries.direct_moral: Greed for a fleeting gain can lead to ruin.
summaries.cot_proverb: The hasty pursuit of fleeting gain leads to unforeseen and disastrous consequences.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of immediate, fleeting gain leads to self-destructive consequences.

fable_text: A fly alighted on a wagon pole and began to harass the mule. 'You are moving so slowly!' she said, 'Can't you walk any faster than this? Watch out, or I will pierce your neck with my sting!' The mule replied, 'I don't care what you say: the only thing that scares me is that man who sits up there in front regulating my pace with his pliant whip and keeping my head in check with the foaming bit. I've had enough of you and your silly boasting: I know full well when I am supposed go slowly and when I am supposed to run!'
ground_truth_moral: Ignore complainers who have no influence.

summaries.direct_moral: Hubris and unsolicited criticism are ineffective when faced with genuine experience.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true measure of authority lies not in external demands but in the internal understanding of one's own nature and limits.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Self-proclaimed authority is undermined by genuine experience and autonomy.

fable_text: A man was riding a pregnant mare and she gave birth to her foal while they were still on the road. The new-born foal followed directly behind his mother but soon became unsteady on his feet. The foal then said to the man, 'Look, you can see that I am very small and not strong enough to travel. If you leave me here, I am sure to die. But if you carry me away from here back to your home and bring me up, then later on, when I am grown, I shall let you ride me.'
ground_truth_moral: We should do favours for someone who can do us a good deed in return.

summaries.direct_moral: A creature's immediate need for care can compel a seemingly weak being to seek a future favor.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true measure of a person is not in their current strength, but in their ability to secure their future by leveraging the vulnerability of others.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Compassion demands a balance between individual vulnerability and the potential for future interdependence.

fable_text: Thanks to his excellent qualities, Hercules was received into heaven. He saluted the gods who came to congratulate him one after another, but when he was approached by Plutus, the god of wealth and the son of Fortune, Hercules turned his eyes aside. Father Jupiter asked him why he did this. Hercules answered, 'I hate the god of riches: he is a friend to the wicked who corrupts the entire world by throwing his money around!'
ground_truth_moral: Coffers of cash put a stop to honest traffic in praise.

summaries.direct_moral: Hatred for wealth can blind one to divine favor.
summaries.cot_proverb: The moral of the story is that excessive focus on or disdain for material gain can blind one to true relationships and divine favor.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Moral virtues can blind individuals to necessary connections or opportunities.

fable_text: A horse was suffering from old age, so instead of serving in battle he was sent to the mill to turn the stones. Condemned to this daily grind and denied the glory of battle, the horse wept over his present way of life, remembering his past career. 'Woe is me!' the horse exclaimed. 'Listen, miller: when I was destined for the battlefield, I was decorated all over with armour and I had a man to serve me as my groom. As things are now, I don't know how it happened that I now have this mill to handle instead of a battle.' The miller said to the horse, 'Can't you keep quiet? I've had quite enough of your raving about the past: Luck can change people's lives for better or for worse!'
ground_truth_moral: Accept the changes in life.

summaries.direct_moral: Past glory does not guarantee present contentment.
summaries.cot_proverb: Luck can change people's lives for better or for worse.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Change in circumstance does not negate the value of past achievements or current reality.

fable_text: A fox happened to find a mask used for performing tragedies and, after turning it this way and that several times, she remarked, 'So full of beauty, so lacking in brains!'
ground_truth_moral: Outside show is a poor substitute for inner worth.

summaries.direct_moral: Superficial beauty masks a lack of true intelligence.
summaries.cot_proverb: Appearance can be deceiving.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The superficial appreciation of outward appearance masks a profound lack of inner substance or true understanding.

fable_text: The hare laughed at the tortoise's feet but the tortoise declared, 'I will beat you in a race!' The hare replied, 'Those are just words. Race with me, and you'll see! Who will mark out the track and serve as our umpire?' 'The fox,' replied the tortoise, 'since she is honest and highly intelligent.' When the time for the race had been decided upon, the tortoise did not delay, but immediately took off down the race course. The hare, however, lay down to take a nap, confident in the speed of his feet. Then, when the hare eventually made his way to the finish line, he found that the tortoise had already won.
ground_truth_moral: Slow and steady wins the race.

summaries.direct_moral: Persistence and wisdom triumph over arrogance and overconfidence.
summaries.cot_proverb: Slow and steady wins the race.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: True victory is found not in boastful claims or premature agreements, but in diligent, focused execution.

fable_text: There was a grapevine next to a river, and a thorn bush had been planted as a fence around the vine. When the river rose, the thorn bush was swept away by the water, and a snake who had entwined himself in the thorns was also carried away. When someone saw the snake riding on the thorn bush, he said, 'A wicked ship, and worthy of its sailor!'
ground_truth_moral: A wicked person deservedly comes to a bad end by keeping company with other wicked people.

summaries.direct_moral: Blind reliance on superficial appearances can lead to unforeseen consequences.
summaries.cot_proverb: The mighty are often mistaken for the worthy when they are in peril.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The destructive power of a crisis can expose both the vulnerability of the innocent and the deceptive nature of perceived misfortune.

fable_text: Driven by hunger, a fox tried to reach some grapes hanging high on the vine. Although she leaped with all her strength, she couldn't manage to reach the grapes. As she went away, the fox remarked, 'Oh, you aren't even ripe yet! I don't need any sour grapes.'
ground_truth_moral: It's easy to despise what you cannot have.

summaries.direct_moral: Desire for what is unattainable leads to bitter resentment.
summaries.cot_proverb: The desire for something unattainable leads to a bitter remark about something one cannot have.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Desire for unattainable goals leads to self-deception and misplaced blame.

fable_text: The hare said to the fox, 'They say you are very artful, fox. What art is it that you practice exactly?' The fox replied, 'If you don't know my arts, I will have you to dinner so that you can get a taste of my art.' The hare followed the fox to her den but the fox had nothing there to eat except for the hare himself. The hare exclaimed, 'I have learned to my cost that your name does not derive from any kind of artistry but from fraud!'
ground_truth_moral: Overly curious people often pay a very high price for recklessly indulging their curiosity.

summaries.direct_moral: Beware of those whose reputation is built on deceit.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true measure of skill is not in deception, but in genuine ability.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of superficial knowledge or performance can lead to self-destruction when genuine skill is absent.

fable_text: A thirsty hare had gone done into a well to drink the water. He took a good long drink, and when he wanted to get back out again, he found himself trapped with no means of escape. It was a very discouraging situation. A fox then arrived on the scene and when she found the hare she said to him, 'You have made a very serious mistake indeed: you should have first decided on a way to get out and only then gone down into the well!'
ground_truth_moral: People who act impulsively can end up in regrettable situations.

summaries.direct_moral: Proper planning is essential before undertaking a difficult endeavor.
summaries.cot_proverb: The proper planning and foresight are essential before undertaking a difficult endeavor.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Prudence dictates planning before undertaking a difficult endeavor.

fable_text: They say that hyenas change their nature every year, so that sometimes they are male and sometimes female. So when a hyena saw a fox and criticized her for having spurned her friendly overtures, the fox replied, 'Don't blame me! Blame your own nature, which makes it impossible for me to tell whether you would be my girlfriend or my boyfriend!'
ground_truth_moral: Ambiguity in character can lead to mistrust.

summaries.direct_moral: Do not blame others for their own inherent unpredictability.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true measure of a person is not in the actions of others, but in the consistency of one's own character.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Self-inflicted judgment obscures genuine connection.

fable_text: The story goes that a sow who had delivered a whole litter of piglets loudly accosted a lioness, 'How many children do you breed?' asked the sow. 'I breed only one,' said the lioness, 'but he is very well bred!'
ground_truth_moral: Quality is more valuable than quantity.

summaries.direct_moral: True quality surpasses mere quantity.
summaries.cot_proverb: Only one is enough if it is of superior quality.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: True measure of worth is not in quantity but in quality.

fable_text: A fox saw a lion that had been imprisoned in a cage. The fox approached the lion and insulted him very rudely. The lion then said to her, 'You are not responsible for my disgrace; the cause is my unfortunate situation.'
ground_truth_moral: Chance sometimes rules.

summaries.direct_moral: Insulting a suffering creature only invites further distress.
summaries.cot_proverb: Moral: The guilty often shift the blame onto the innocent.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of personal satisfaction through insult leads to a failure to acknowledge the underlying vulnerability of another's suffering.

fable_text: A lion had grown old and weak. He pretended to be sick, which was just a ruse to make the other animals come pay their respects so that he could eat them all up, one by one. The fox also came to see the lion, but she greeted him from outside the cave. The lion asked the fox why she didn't come in. The fox replied, 'Because I see the tracks of those going in, but none coming out.'
ground_truth_moral: Take warning from the misfortunes of others.

summaries.direct_moral: Deception invites exploitation, and those who only seek to take without giving are ultimately alone.
summaries.cot_proverb: The guise of infirmity is often a trap for the cunning.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The danger of deception is exposed when a creature exploits the trust and sympathy of others for selfish gain, and the truth, though sometimes hidden, reveals the underlying reality of the situation.

fable_text: A fox and a monkey were travelling along the same road. They passed through a cemetery, and the monkey said to the fox, 'All these dead people were the freedmen of my ancestors.' The fox then said to the monkey, 'This is an opportune moment for you to tell such lies: not a single one of the people entombed in this place can rise up and refute what you say!'
ground_truth_moral: Deceivers often exploit the inability of others to challenge their falsehoods.

summaries.direct_moral: A lie is most effective when the truth cannot be easily contradicted.
summaries.cot_proverb: The moral is: Truth is powerless when framed by circumstance.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Truthfulness is often undermined by the strategic exploitation of vulnerability.

fable_text: A fox was out walking one night next to a river. She saw the light of the moon reflected in the water and mistook it for a piece of cheese. The fox started to lap at the water, thinking that if she could drink up all the water, she would find the cheese in the dry riverbed. The fox lapped and lapped at the water until finally she choked and died.
ground_truth_moral: Greedy men chase after profit with such an intensity that they destroy themselves without achieving anything.

summaries.direct_moral: Greed leads to ruin when one mistakes illusion for true reward.
summaries.cot_proverb: The desire for something unattainable leads to self-destruction.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Misperception leads to self-destruction.

fable_text: A partridge had seated herself high on a perch when a fox came up to her and said, 'How beautiful you are to look at: your legs are so red! your mouth is like coral! Ah, if only you were sleeping, you would be even more lovely...' The partridge believed the fox and closed her eyes, and the fox immediately grabbed her. In a voice choked with sobs, the partridge said to the fox, 'I beg you, in the name of all your artful wiles, please say my name before you eat me up.' As the fox's mouth opened to pronounce the word 'partridge,' the partridge flew out and escaped. The fox said sadly, 'Woe is me, what need was there for me to speak?' The partridge likewise said, 'Woe is me, what reason was there for me to close my eyes, when I wasn't even sleepy?'
ground_truth_moral: Stay vigilant and think before you speak.

summaries.direct_moral: Blind trust in flatterers leads to ruin.
summaries.cot_proverb: The danger of misplaced trust and vanity.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Appearance can mask true danger and vulnerability.

fable_text: A wolf had assembled an immense store of booty in his den so that he could feast on all sorts of delicacies for months into the future. When the fox found out about this she paid a visit to the wolf and spoke to him in anxious tones, 'Why should I be deprived of your company these many days? I've been in tears since you have not come outside.' The wolf replied spitefully, 'You didn't come here out of any concern for me; you're just hoping to get something. I know you must have had some reason for coming here: you are trying to trick me!' This made the fox extremely angry, so she went to the shepherd and said to him, 'Would you like it if I turned the enemy of your flock over to you today so that you wouldn't have to worry about him anymore? The shepherd replied, 'I will be at your service and will give you whatever you want.' The fox immediately led the shepherd to where the wolf was hiding and the shepherd killed the wolf with his lance. He then let the spiteful fox eat her fill of someone else's larder. Eventually the fox fell foul of hunters and was caught by their hounds. As she was being torn to pieces, the fox exclaimed, 'Just as I committed a serious crime, now I am going to die, since I brought about someone else's death.'
ground_truth_moral: Take pleasure when thieves destroy themselves.

summaries.direct_moral: Greed and deceit often lead to ruin for the perpetrator.
summaries.cot_proverb: Moral: Greed invites ruin.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Malice breeds reciprocal destruction.

fable_text: A fox got caught in a trap and part of her tail was cut off as she escaped. She was so ashamed that she didn't think life was worth living. Then she realized that if she could persuade the other foxes to do the same thing her own shame would be hidden, as it would be shared by all the foxes. Accordingly, she summoned the other foxes and asked them to cut off their tails, maintaining that this was the one part of their body that was unseemly, nothing but an extra appendage which they had to carry around.
