One of the other foxes scoffed in reply, 'If it weren't for the fact that it is in your interest to do this thing, you never would have proposed it!'
ground_truth_moral: Distrust advice from someone who stands to gain.

summaries.direct_moral: Shame can be manipulated to gain acceptance, but true conviction is revealed by self-interest.
summaries.cot_proverb: The greater the shame, the more desperate individuals are to find shared transgression to alleviate personal burden, even if it means exploiting others.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Self-interest can corrupt moral judgment when framed as a means of collective justification.

fable_text: The fox had never seen a lion before, so when she happened to meet the lion for the first time she all but died of fright. The second time she saw him, she was still afraid, but not as much as before. The third time, the fox was bold enough to go right up to the lion and speak to him.
ground_truth_moral: Familiarity makes it easy to confront even frightening situations.

summaries.direct_moral: Courage grows with repeated experience.
summaries.cot_proverb: The greatest fear often diminishes with repeated exposure, leading to courage through repeated experience.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Courage is developed through repeated exposure to fear.

fable_text: The lion had grown old and sick and was lying in his cave. All the animals, except for the fox, had come to visit their king. The wolf seized this opportunity to denounce the fox in front of the lion, complaining that the fox showed no respect for the lion, who was the common master of them all. Indeed, the fox had not even come to pay the ailing lion a visit! The fox arrived just in time to hear the end of the wolf's speech. The lion roared at the fox, but the fox asked for a chance to explain herself. 'After all,' said the fox, 'which one of all the animals assembled here has helped you as I have, travelling all over the world in order to seek out and discover from the doctors a remedy for your illness?' The lion ordered the fox to describe the remedy immediately, and the fox replied, 'You must flay a living wolf and wrap yourself in his skin while it is still warm.' When the wolf had been killed, the fox laughed and said, 'It is better to put your master in a good mood, not a bad one.'
ground_truth_moral: Someone who plots against others falls into his own trap.

summaries.direct_moral: A clever individual can find a way to benefit even from a difficult situation.
summaries.cot_proverb: The timely intervention of a clever individual can silence malicious accusations and reveal the true nature of a conflict.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Respect and utility are often secondary to self-interest in social dynamics.

fable_text: Tiberius Caesar was on his way to Naples and had arrived at his estate in Misenum which had been built by Lucullus on a high hill overlooking the Sicilian sea on one side and the Tuscan sea on the other. When Caesar was walking about in the cheerful greenery, one of his household stewards turned up, dressed in a fancy fringed tunic of Egyptian cotton hanging down from his shoulders. The man began to sprinkle the sizzling hot ground with water from a wooden basin, making a great show of his diligence as Caesar's attendant, but everyone just laughed at him. The man then ran ahead to the next walkway, using some shortcuts known only to himself, and he started settling the dust in that spot as well. When Caesar recognized the man and realized what he was doing, he said, 'Hey you!' The man scampered up to Caesar, excited at the joyful prospect of what seemed a sure reward. Then Caesar's majestic person made the following joke: 'You have not accomplished much and your efforts have come to naught; if you want me to give you the slap that makes you a freedman, it will cost you much more than that!'
ground_truth_moral: Beware of those who appear busy but achieve little of value.

summaries.direct_moral: Showing false diligence only invites ridicule and greater loss.
summaries.cot_proverb: "Vanity is the reward of a fool."
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of superficial achievement and false diligence often leads to disappointment when genuine merit is overlooked.

fable_text: The Lion hearing an odd kind of a hollow voice, and seeing nobody, started up. He listened again; the voice continued, and he shook with fear. At last seeing a Frog crawl out of the lake, and finding that the noise proceeded from that little creature, he spurned it to pieces with his feet.
ground_truth_moral: Imaginary fears are the worst.

summaries.direct_moral: Prideful judgment often leads to the destruction of the innocent.
summaries.cot_proverb: The mighty are often quick to judge and destroy what they cannot comprehend.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Moral judgment is flawed when fear or assumption replaces evidence, leading to unjust harm.

fable_text: There were two frogs who lived near one another. One lived in a deep pond that was far away from the road, but the other lived near the road where there was only a small amount of water. The frog who lived in the deep pond advised the other frog to move in with him so that they could share the pond between them and live a life that was less fraught with danger. The roadside frog refused the offer, saying that he had become accustomed to his home and couldn't tear himself away. Not long afterwards, he was crushed under the wheel of a passing wagon.
ground_truth_moral: People who stubbornly cling to unwise choices lose everything before they know it.

summaries.direct_moral: Attachment to comfort can lead to greater peril than necessary caution.
summaries.cot_proverb: The comfort of the familiar, even when precarious, can be a trap that leads to greater peril than the unknown but potentially safer alternative.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Self-preservation can override the wisdom of communal risk.

fable_text: A mouse asked a frog to help her get across the river. The frog tied the mouse's front leg to her own back leg using a piece of string and they swam out to the middle of the stream. The frog then turned traitor and plunged down into the water, dragging the mouse along with her. The mouse's dead body floated up to the surface and was drifting along when a kite flew by and noticed something he could snatch. When he grabbed the mouse he also carried off her friend the frog. Thus the treacherous frog who had betrayed the mouse's life was likewise killed and eaten.
ground_truth_moral: People who do harm to others destroy themselves in the bargain.

summaries.direct_moral: Betrayal invites reciprocal ruin.
summaries.cot_proverb: The serpent that strikes his prey also wounds himself.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Betrayal invites reciprocal destruction.

fable_text: A frog looked out from her pond and saw a battle taking place between the bulls. 'Oh no!' she said, 'There is terrible danger in store for us.' Another frog asked her why she said this, since the bulls were fighting for control of the herd in their home far away from the frogs. The first frog explained, 'While their habitat may be separate from ours and our species not the same, the bull who is driven from the lordship of the meadow will come to find a secret hiding place here in the marsh, crushing us beneath his heavy hooves. That is why their frenzy is a matter of life and death for us!'
ground_truth_moral: Poor folk suffer when the high and mighty are at war with one another.

summaries.direct_moral: Fear of others' conflict can bring unforeseen danger to oneself.
summaries.cot_proverb: The moral is that the troubles of others can pose a real threat to oneself.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The danger faced by the frogs is a direct consequence of the conflict of another group, demonstrating that the actions of others can have unforeseen and devastating consequences for an innocent party, even when the conflict is geographically distant.

fable_text: There was a viper who used to go to a certain spring in order to drink but he was driven away by a water-snake who was furious that the viper would not just stay on his own turf instead of encroaching on the water-snake's territory. Their dispute grew more and more fierce and finally the two of them decided to fight it out, agreeing that both the water and the land would be awarded to the winner. When the day for the fight had been decided, the frogs came to the viper and, since they hated the water-snake, they offered to be his allies and come to his aid during the battle. But when the battle began and the viper grappled with the water-snake, the frogs just sat there croaking, since they were not able to do anything else. In the end, the viper was victorious but he was furious with the frogs since they had failed to come to his aid as they had promised and, what was worse, they had sat there singing songs while he was doing battle. The frogs then said to the viper, 'But you should have known that we had nothing to offer you except the sound of our voices!'
ground_truth_moral: When you need someone to lend a hand, mere words are no help at all.

summaries.direct_moral: Respect for boundaries is necessary, but false alliances prove useless in a true conflict.
summaries.cot_proverb: The promise of alliance proved hollow when the promised support failed to materialize, revealing that words without substance are worthless in a true conflict.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of dominance leads to the failure of alliances when self-interest overrides commitment.

fable_text: There were two frogs whose pond had dried up, so they went looking for a new place to live. When they came to a well, one of them thought that they should jump in immediately, but the other one said, 'Wait: what if the water were to dry up here too; how would we be able to get back out again?'
ground_truth_moral: Look before you leap.

summaries.direct_moral: Prudence dictates pausing to consider the consequences before acting.
summaries.cot_proverb: Caution precedes action.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Prudence in action prevents rash decisions that might lead to further peril.

fable_text: When Zeus had fashioned man and woman, he ordered Hermes to take them to Gaia, the Earth, and to show them how to obtain food by digging in the ground. At first, the Earth refused to cooperate in Hermes' mission. Hermes then compelled her, saying that Zeus had ordered her to do so. Earth replied, 'Then let them dig as much as they like, but they will pay for it with groans and tears!'
ground_truth_moral: Easy gains often come with hidden costs and hardships.

summaries.direct_moral: Persistence in demanding others to comply will only elicit reluctant compliance.
summaries.cot_proverb: The cost of forcing nature is suffering.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Coercion without genuine cooperation breeds resentment and exploitation.

fable_text: The vine was covered with clusters of grapes and its shoots were as vigorous as its fruit. A goat began to eat the vine, indulging his outrageous appetite. He had already wreaked havoc on the fresh shoots when the vine said to him, 'You will pay for this outrage! The time will soon come when you will be sent as a holy victim to be sacrificed, and I myself will be the one to supply the wine for the libation!'
ground_truth_moral: The things that a person does to others will happen to him in turn.

summaries.direct_moral: Greed and excess will inevitably lead to severe retribution.
summaries.cot_proverb: The greedy exploitation of a vulnerable resource will inevitably lead to retribution from the victim.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Unchecked desire inevitably leads to severe and deserved retribution.

fable_text: When the she-goats had persuaded Jupiter to give them beards, the male goats were extremely upset and began to complain that the women now had the same degree of dignity that they did. 'Come now,' said Jupiter, 'let the women enjoy this vain source of pride and stake their claim to the badges of your office so long as they cannot rival you in strength!'
ground_truth_moral: Bear that those who are inferior to you in merit should be like you in outside appearances.

summaries.direct_moral: Do not compete with others for pride when you are not equal in ability.
summaries.cot_proverb: The greater the pride of one group, the more they attempt to diminish the pride of another group to elevate themselves.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of status leads to the devaluation of inherent worth when external validation is prioritized over genuine capability.

fable_text: Some swans and some geese became friends and went out onto the meadows. As they were feeding contentedly together, they were discovered by hunters. The swans, owing to the swiftness of their bodies, were immediately able to take wing and fly away. The geese, however, were naturally more heavy and so they were left behind and caught by the hunters.
ground_truth_moral: Beware of enterprizes where the risks are not equal.

summaries.direct_moral: Swiftness can save life, but lack of agility can lead to peril.
summaries.cot_proverb: Moral: Swiftness can save, but lack of agility can lead to peril.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Natural differences in capability determine survival outcomes.

fable_text: A stork had gone to her usual pond and saw a goose there who kept plunging down deep into the water. The stork then asked the goose what she was doing and the goose replied, 'We geese go down into the muddy bottom of the pond to look for food and to escape the hawk's attack.' The stork said, 'I am stronger than the hawk! You should be friends with me, and I will allow you to scoff at that other bird!' The goose agreed, and not long afterwards she called on the stork to come help her. The goose had not plunged into the water, so the hawk immediately swooped down and caught her up in his talons. As the hawk was about to devour her, the goose replied, 'A wretched death awaits anyone who puts his trust in such a worthless defender!'
ground_truth_moral: Do not expect to be defended by someone who cannot offer any protection.

summaries.direct_moral: Blind loyalty to a false protector invites greater peril.
summaries.cot_proverb: A worthless friend will betray you when it is most needed.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Blind trust in a weak protector invites greater peril.

fable_text: The halcyon is a bird who is fond of deserted places and who always lives on the sea. They say that she makes her nest on the rocky cliffs of the coast in order to protect herself from human hunters. So when a certain halcyon was about to lay her eggs, she went to a promontory and found a rock jutting out towards the sea and decided to make her nest there. But when she went to look for food, it happened that the sea swelled under the blustering wind and reached as high as the halcyon's home and flooded the nest, killing her chicks. When the halcyon returned and saw what had happened, she said, 'What a fool I was to have protected myself against a plot hatched on the land by taking refuge here on the sea, when it is the sea that has utterly betrayed me!'
ground_truth_moral: Sometimes, the greatest threats come from those you trust.

summaries.direct_moral: Overconfidence in defense against a greater, uncontrollable force leads to ruin.
summaries.cot_proverb: The shield you build against one danger may invite a greater one.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Self-imposed isolation can lead to vulnerability when facing unforeseen, overwhelming forces.

fable_text: There was a hunter who had caught a hare and was carrying it home. As he went along his way, he met a man on horseback who asked him for the hare, pretending that he wanted to buy it. As soon as he got the hare from the hunter, the horseman immediately took off at a gallop. The hunter began to pursue the horseman thinking that he might catch up with him. When the horseman finally disappeared into the distance, the hunter reluctantly said, 'Go ahead then! That hare is my gift to you.'
ground_truth_moral: A fool will make excuses for foolish behavior.

summaries.direct_moral: Greed often leads to the loss of what one possesses.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true value of a gift is often revealed by the recipient's eagerness to acquire it, even when the giver is reluctant to relinquish it.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit and subsequent relinquishing of the prey demonstrate that the desire for possession can sometimes lead to a temporary conflict, but ultimately, a recognition of the other's right or the futility of continued struggle leads to a compassionate resolution.

fable_text: A certain hare happened to see a stag adorned with great branching horns. The hare complained to Jupiter that the hares were weak, defective creatures who were not feared by any of the other animals. Therefore, he wanted Jupiter to give him horns like those of the stag both for his own protection and to enhance his appearance. Jupiter told the hare that he would not be able to carry the heavy weight of the horns, but the hare replied that he was prepared to carry such horns with pride. Jupiter therefore commanded that the hare's head should be crowned with a pair of big, branching horns, but the hare was so weighed down by his burden that he wasn't able to run. As a result, the shepherds captured and killed him.
ground_truth_moral: It is often the case that people are greedy for things which they think will bring them honour, but which only result in their death and destruction.

summaries.direct_moral: Excessive desire for power or appearance can lead to downfall.
summaries.cot_proverb: The moral is: Vanity invites ruin.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Excessive desire for status or power, when pursued without considering the actual capacity to bear the responsibility, leads to self-destruction.

fable_text: There was a rooster who was always quarrelling with another rooster, so he asked the hawk to judge their dispute. The rooster was actually hoping that when he brought the other rooster before the judge, the judge would devour him. Yet when they came before the judge to make their arguments, the hawk grabbed the rooster who had originally brought the case to court. The rooster shouted, 'Not me! You want the one who is running away!' The hawk replied, 'Abandon all hope of being released from my talons today! Justice demands that you yourself should suffer the punishment that you intended for another.'
ground_truth_moral: Those who scheme against others may ultimately fall victim to their own plots.

summaries.direct_moral: Do not seek to inflict harm on others, for justice will return the injury to the aggressor.
summaries.cot_proverb: The law of retributive justice is blind to intent.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Justice demands that one should not seek to inflict harm upon another while expecting to escape consequence.

fable_text: A nightingale, sitting aloft upon an oak and singing according to his wont, was seen by a Hawk who, being in need of food, swooped down and seized him. The Nightingale, about to lose his life, earnestly begged the Hawk to let him go, saying that he was not big enough to satisfy the hunger of a Hawk who, if he wanted food, ought to pursue the larger birds. The Hawk, interrupting him, said: 'I should indeed have lost my senses if I should let go food ready in my hand, for the sake of pursuing birds which are not yet even within sight.'
ground_truth_moral: Do not neglect small advantages in hopes of better ones.

summaries.direct_moral: Do not seek what is beyond your reach when a lesser gain is offered.
summaries.cot_proverb: "Do not seek to appease a predator with pleas for mercy; sometimes, the pursuit of immediate gain outweighs the consideration for the vulnerable."
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The moral is that self-preservation often conflicts with the desperate plea for mercy when faced with overwhelming power.

fable_text: There was a widow who had a hen that laid one egg each and every day. The woman then began to feed the hen more lavishly, thinking that if the hen ate more grain she would lay two eggs. The hen got so fat from eating all the food that she stopped laying eggs entirely.
ground_truth_moral: People who grasp at more than they need lose the little that they held in their hands.

summaries.direct_moral: Excessive desire for more often leads to a loss of what is already possessed.
summaries.cot_proverb: Greed for an increased yield often results in the complete cessation of the original benefit.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Excessive desire for gain can lead to the destruction of the very source of that gain.

fable_text: A hen came across the eggs of a snake and devoted herself to them, settling atop the eggs and brooding on them. A swallow saw what the hen was doing and said, 'O you stupid, senseless creature! They will destroy you first of all and then destroy everyone around you!'
ground_truth_moral: We should never put our trust in a wicked man, even if he seems to be completely innocuous.

summaries.direct_moral: Blind devotion to a destructive thing invites ruin for oneself and others.
summaries.cot_proverb: "A fool's devotion invites ruin for all."
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Blind devotion to a destructive object leads to self-inflicted peril and subsequent harm to others.

fable_text: Hermes wanted to know how much people valued him, so he assumed a human form and went into a sculptor's workshop. He saw there a statue of Zeus and he asked how much it cost. The man said that it cost a drachma. Hermes smiled, and asked how much the statue of Hera would be. The man named a still higher price. When Hermes saw a statue of himself, he expected that he would be reckoned at an even higher price, since he delivered the messages of the gods and brought profit to mankind. But when he asked how much the statue of Hermes would cost, the sculptor replied, 'If you buy those other two, I'll throw this one in for free!'
ground_truth_moral: Vanity can blind one to their true worth in the eyes of others.

summaries.direct_moral: True value is often found in relationships rather than in perceived importance.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true measure of worth is not in the perceived service or inherent value, but in the context of comparison and perceived marketability.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The true measure of worth is not found in the perceived importance of a role, but in the context of the relationship and the generosity of the giver.

fable_text: After Zeus had fashioned the human race, he ordered Hermes to give them intelligence. Hermes divided intelligence into equal portions and then applied it to each person. The result was that short people became wise, since they were more completely suffused with the standard dose of intelligence, while the tall people turned out stupid, since the potion that was poured into their bodies did not even reach as high as their knees.
ground_truth_moral: Physical stature does not guarantee wisdom or intelligence.

summaries.direct_moral: True wisdom is not determined by stature, but by the proper distribution of knowledge.
summaries.cot_proverb: The measure of wisdom is not in stature, but in the depth of the infusion.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Uneven distribution of inherent capacity leads to unequal outcomes.

fable_text: A man was chopping wood by a certain river when he dropped his axe and it was carried away by the current. The man then sat down on the riverbank and began to weep. The god Hermes finally took pity on the man and appeared before him. When Hermes learned the reason for his sorrow, he brought up a golden axe and asked whether that was the man's axe. The man said that it was not his. A second time, Hermes brought up a silver axe, and again asked the man if this was the axe he had lost but the man said that it was not. The third time Hermes brought up the axe that the man had lost and when the man recognized his axe, Hermes rewarded the man's honesty by giving all of the axes to him as a gift. The man took the axes and went to tell his friends what had happened. One of the men was jealous and wanted to do the same thing, so he took his axe and went to the river. He began chopping some wood and then intentionally let his axe fall into the whirling waters. As he was weeping, Hermes appeared and asked him what had happened, and the man said that he had lost his axe. When Hermes brought up the golden axe and asked the man if that was the axe he had lost, the greedy man got excited and said that it was the one. Not only did the man fail to receive any gifts from the god, he didn't even retrieve his own axe.
ground_truth_moral: Honesty is the best policy.

summaries.direct_moral: Honesty is rewarded, but greed leads to further loss.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true value of honesty is often overlooked by greed.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Honesty is rewarded while deceit invites further loss.

fable_text: A traveller who needed to make a long journey vowed that if he found anything, he would give half of it to Hermes. When he came across a bag full of dates and almonds he grabbed the bag and ate the almonds and dates. He then placed the pits of the dates and the shells of the almonds upon an altar and said 'You have what was promised you, O Hermes: I have saved the outsides and the insides for you!'
ground_truth_moral: The promises of the greedy are often empty and insincere.

summaries.direct_moral: True fulfillment is found in fulfilling a promise, even in unexpected ways.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true reward is often found in the effort, even when the immediate desire is to claim the whole.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The fulfillment of a promise leads to a selective fulfillment that benefits the self while appearing to satisfy the commitment to another.

fable_text: A man fashioned a Hermes out of wood and carried it to the market to put it up for sale but no customers approached him. In order to attract some buyers the man began to shout that he was selling a wish-fulfilling god who brought profit to its owner. 'Hey you,' someone said, 'why are you putting such a thing up for sale, instead of enjoying its benefits yourself?' The man answered, 'I am in need of some immediate benefits, but this god happens to take his time when distributing profits!'
ground_truth_moral: When you lie, you often have to expand the lie.

summaries.direct_moral: True value is found in the inherent worth of a thing rather than in inflated claims.
summaries.cot_proverb: The desire for external gain often blinds one to the value of what is already possessed.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Self-interest often leads to the misdirection of genuine value.

fable_text: A man kept a hero shrine in his house and made expensive sacrifices to it. Since he was constantly paying out money and spending great sums on the offerings, the god came to him one night and said: 'Look here, stop squandering your wealth: if you spend everything and become poor, you'll put the blame on me!'
ground_truth_moral: When people suffer misfortune because of their own foolishness, they often blame the gods.

summaries.direct_moral: Excessive devotion and wasteful spending invite divine retribution.
summaries.cot_proverb: The fool's folly is to overcommit to devotion, risking ruin for the sake of appeasing an idol.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Excessive devotion and material expenditure can lead to a loss of self-control and the imposition of blame onto external forces.

fable_text: The river saw that a cowhide was being swept along by his current, so he said to her, 'What is your name?' The cowhide answered, 'They call me Tough. ' Splashing the hide with his waves, the river said, 'You better get yourself another name, since I am going to soften you up!'
ground_truth_moral: Life's disasters can often bring boastful and presumptuous people back down to earth.

summaries.direct_moral: One should adapt to their circumstances or identity to survive.
summaries.cot_proverb: The more you try to resist change, the more you are subjected to it.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The need for self-definition and resilience is often undermined by external forces that seek to diminish or change the individual's identity.

fable_text: A mountain had gone into labour and was groaning terribly. Such rumours excited great expectations all over the country. In the end, however, the mountain gave birth to a mouse.
ground_truth_moral: Don't promise big things if you can't deliver.

summaries.direct_moral: Great expectations often lead to a disappointing reality.
summaries.cot_proverb: The height of expectation invites a meager reward.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Unfulfilled expectations often result in a disappointing reality.

fable_text: A certain man took a deposit from a friend but intended to keep it for himself. When the depositor then summoned him to swear an oath regarding the deposit, he realized the danger he was in and prepared to leave the city and go to his farm. When he reached the city gates, he saw a lame man who was also on his way out of town. He asked the man who he was and where he was going. The man said that he was the god named Oath and that he was on his way to track down wicked people. The man then asked Oath how often he revisited each city. Oath replied, 'I come back after forty years, or sometimes thirty.' Accordingly, on the very next day the man did not hesitate to swear an oath that he had never received the deposit. But then the man ran into Oath, who dragged him off to the edge of a cliff. The man asked Oath how he could have said that he wasn't coming back for another thirty years when in fact he didn't even grant him a single day's reprieve. Oath explained, 'You also need to know that if somebody intends to provoke me, I am accustomed to come back again the very same day.'
ground_truth_moral: There is no fixed day on which wicked people are punished.

summaries.direct_moral: A promise made under duress is often broken when the accuser seeks immediate retribution.
summaries.cot_proverb: The deeper the commitment to a promise, the more severe the consequences for breaking it.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The danger of deceit is often met with a more severe, yet equally determined, form of retribution.

fable_text: As he was crossing through a field, a wolf found some barley. Since wolves don't eat barley, he ignored it and continued on his way. The wolf then ran into a horse. He led the horse into the field and showed him the barley, saying that instead of eating the barley himself, he had saved it for the horse, since he liked to hear the sound of the horse's teeth grinding together. The horse then said to the wolf, 'Look here, if you wolves ate barley, you would never have put the pleasure of your ears before your stomach!'
ground_truth_moral: Nobody believes people who are inherently wicked, even if they pretend to be good-natured.

summaries.direct_moral: Self-interest often leads to a situation where one's own desires are overlooked in favor of exploiting another's.
summaries.cot_proverb: The pleasure of the ear is often prioritized over practical necessity or immediate gratification.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Self-interest often blinds individuals to the true nature of their own motivations and the motivations of others.

fable_text: A certain hunter saw a wolf attacking a flock and tearing to pieces as many of the sheep as he could. The hunter skilfully hunted down the wolf and set his dogs on him. Then he shouted at the wolf, 'O you terrible beast, where is that former strength of yours? You cannot even withstand the attack of these dogs!'
ground_truth_moral: Each man makes his reputation in the field which is his specialty.

summaries.direct_moral: True strength is revealed when facing a superior foe with unexpected allies.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true measure of strength is not in brute force alone, but in the ability to maintain one's former capability when faced with new challenges.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The efficacy of a superior force is often revealed not by its brute strength alone, but by its ability to demonstrate a loss of former capability when confronted by a new, unexpected challenge.

fable_text: There was a boy singing sweet serenades in the middle of the night. A woman heard him and got up from bed to peep out the window at him. When she saw the boy, who looked very beautiful in the shining moonlight, she left her husband asleep and went downstairs and out the door. She then met the boy in the street and satisfied herself completely. All of a sudden her husband woke up and wanted to find out where his wife had gone. Not finding her inside the house, he didn't just stand there gaping but instead followed her outside and said, 'It's alright. Go ahead and persuade the boy to sleep in our house.' So he took the boy and brought him inside. He didn't have any trouble after that, and joined in whenever the two of them wanted to do something.
ground_truth_moral: Acceptance can lead to unexpected harmony.

summaries.direct_moral: Unchecked desire often leads to unforeseen and compromising consequences.
summaries.cot_proverb: The temptation of beauty leads to ruin, even when disguised by apparent innocence.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of desire often leads to unforeseen consequences and the eventual entanglement of parties in a complex, morally ambiguous arrangement.

fable_text: They say that the hyena has a double nature: for a period of time the hyena is male, and then later on she is female. The story goes that when a male hyena was treating a female badly, she said to him, 'Listen here: remember how things used to be, and don't forget that I will be a male hyena the next time around!'
ground_truth_moral: People who have been judged in the past can later on be in a position to judge their former teachers.

summaries.direct_moral: A person's current behavior can change based on their perceived status or future circumstances.
summaries.cot_proverb: The seed of past grievances can be used to justify present mistreatment.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The danger of past grievances and shifting power dynamics can lead to unpredictable and potentially harmful behavior.

fable_text: A man caught a jackdaw and tied the bird's foot with a piece of string so that he could give the bird to his children as a present. The jackdaw, however, could not stand to live in human society, so when they let him loose for just a moment, he ran away. But when he got back to his nest, the string became entangled in the branches, so that the jackdaw was unable to fly. As he was dying, the bird said to himself, 'How stupid of me! Since I could not stand being a slave in human society, I have brought about my own death.'
ground_truth_moral: It is not the place, nor the condition, but the mind alone that is responsible for our happiness.

summaries.direct_moral: Desire for freedom can lead to self-inflicted ruin.
summaries.cot_proverb: The moral of the story is that those who seek to control or manipulate others for selfish purposes often find their own plans unraveling.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Self-imposed limitations stemming from a desire for freedom can lead to self-destruction.

fable_text: One day the lion pretended to be sick and went limping up to the unicorn, his chief enemy. He greeted the unicorn and said to him, 'Let us put aside all that we have done in the past, because I am no longer able to harm anyone at all. As you can see, I am old and suffering from various ailments. But before I die, I would very much like to speak with my wife who is out in the desert. So, kind sir, if I might be so bold, I would like to borrow your horn to use as a walking stick on my journey since it is just the right length and very sturdy. I promise to return it to you as soon as I reach my wife; I give you my word.' The unicorn believed the lion and pitied his feigned distress, so he loaned the lion his horn and was thus left defenceless. The lion then inflicted a serious wound on the unicorn and laid him low. The unicorn said, 'You are guilty not so much of cruelty as of treachery, since you repaid my favour with wickedness and betrayed the promise you made me.' The lion said, 'You fool, don't you know that the saying: The man who prolongs his enemy's life takes something from his own; clemency does not entail showing mercy to one's enemies.' The unicorn replied, 'You traitor, don't you know that in the same book it is written: Let the victory which we contrive by the sword be an honourable victory or no victory at all; let not posterity read that I won by guile; no perfidy should obscure my triumph.'
ground_truth_moral: Do not ever trust your enemy. Always protect yourself from him, even if he comes to you humble and supplicating.

summaries.direct_moral: Pretending weakness to gain advantage ultimately leads to betrayal and ruin.
summaries.cot_proverb: The man who seeks advantage from an enemy's weakness will ultimately invite greater harm upon himself.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Treachery repaid kindness with harm.

fable_text: A kid had wandered off from the flock and was being chased by a wolf. Unable to get away, the kid fell into the wolf's clutches so she turned to him and said, 'I know for a fact that I am about to become your dinner, but I would like to die with dignity: please play the flute for me so that I can do a little dance.' The wolf played the flute and the kid danced to the music. The sound alerted the shepherd's dogs who attacked the wolf and chased him away. As the wolf ran off he said to himself, 'It serves me right for trying to be a musician instead of a butcher!'
ground_truth_moral: Do not let anything turn you from your purpose.

summaries.direct_moral: Appearance of vulnerability can sometimes lead to unexpected salvation.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true measure of courage is not in brute force, but in finding a clever way to turn a dire situation into an opportunity.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Courageous appeal can sometimes achieve unexpected positive outcomes.

fable_text: The Goat, going abroad to feed, shut up her young Kid at borne, charging him to bolt the door fast, and open it to nobody, till she herself should return. The Wolf, who lay lurking just by, heard this charge given; and soon after came and knocked at the door, counterfeiting the voice of the Goat, and desiring to be admitted. The Kid, looking out at a window, and finding the cheat, bid him go about his business; for, however he might imitate a Goat's voice, yet he appeared too much like a Wolf to be trusted.
ground_truth_moral: A hypocrite can usually be found out.

summaries.direct_moral: Imitation of a trusted voice cannot mask a deceptive nature.
summaries.cot_proverb: The imitation of a trusted voice can betray a true nature.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Trustworthiness is diminished by deceptive imitation.

fable_text: A seagull having bolted down too large a fish, burst its deep gullet-bag and lay down on the shore to die. A Kite saw him and exclaimed: 'You richly deserve your fate; for a bird of the air has no business to seek its food from the sea.'
ground_truth_moral: Every man should be content to mind his own business.

summaries.direct_moral: A creature should not presume to exploit a domain beyond its natural capacity.
summaries.cot_proverb: The greedy often find themselves undone by their own desires.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Moral hubris invites inevitable downfall, regardless of the means employed.

fable_text: A wolf had been mauled by dogs and had cast himself down on the ground. Because of his injuries, the wolf could not go in search of food, so when he saw a sheep, he begged her to bring him something to drink from the river that ran nearby. 'If you just give me something to drink,' said the wolf, 'I will find myself something to eat.' The sheep replied, 'But if I give you with something to drink, then you will make me your dinner as well!'
ground_truth_moral: Hypocritical speeches are easily seen through.

summaries.direct_moral: Desperation breeds a reciprocal exploitation of vulnerability.
summaries.cot_proverb: "He who seeks to gain from another's need invites a reciprocal loss."
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Mutual exploitation leads to a destructive cycle of self-interest.

fable_text: Thieves broke into a certain house and didn't find anything inside except a rooster. The thieves grabbed the rooster and made their escape. Later, when they were ready to kill him, the rooster begged the thieves to let him go, claiming that he was useful to people because he woke them to go about their tasks in the dark. The thieves said, 'All the more reason to kill you: when you wake them up, you prevent us from robbing their houses!'
ground_truth_moral: The safeguards of virtue are hateful to those with evil intentions.

summaries.direct_moral: A creature's usefulness can sometimes be mistaken for a reason to harm it.
summaries.cot_proverb: The cost of a creature's utility is its vulnerability to those who seek to exploit it.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of immediate gain leads to a failure to recognize the indirect, yet vital, value of a thing, which can ultimately be used to prevent a greater harm.

fable_text: A pigeon had grown very thirsty, so she flew from place to place looking for some water to drink. She saw a water jar painted on a wall and thought that it was actually full of water, so she flew right into the wall in order to take a drink. As the pigeon collided with the wall, she brought her life abruptly to an end. As she drew her last breath, the pigeon said to herself, 'What a wretched and unlucky creature I am! I didn't even suspect that looking for something to drink could bring about my own demise.'
ground_truth_moral: Caution is far superior to reckless haste and hurry.

summaries.direct_moral: Overconfidence in a desperate situation can lead to ruin.
summaries.cot_proverb: Looking for water can lead to one's own ruin if one fails to discern true value from illusion.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The danger of misplaced trust and assumption leads to self-inflicted ruin.

fable_text: A lark fell into a snare and sang a lament, 'Woe is me, wretched and unlucky bird that I am! I have brought about my own demise not for gold or silver or some object of value, but merely for a tiny bit of food.'
ground_truth_moral: People are willing to risk their lives for the sake of some petty profit.

summaries.direct_moral: Excessive desire for trivial gain leads to self-inflicted ruin.
summaries.cot_proverb: The pursuit of petty gain invites downfall.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Self-inflicted suffering arises from misplaced focus on trivial gain.

fable_text: A Lark, caught in a snare, pleaded earnestly with the Fowler for her life. 'What have I done that I must die?' said she; 'I have stolen neither gold nor silver, but only a grain of corn to satisfy my hunger.' The Man, without deigning any reply, twisted her neck and threw her into his sack.
ground_truth_moral: When your adversary is also your judge, don't bother arguing.

summaries.direct_moral: Desperation does not excuse the consequence of one's own actions.
summaries.cot_proverb: Moral: Hunger makes a creature a target for exploitation.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Self-pity and appeal to sympathy do not alter the reality of one's predicament or the power dynamics between individuals.

fable_text: There were two men travelling together: one was a liar and the other always told the truth. Their journey led them to the land of the monkeys. There was a whole crowd of monkeys there and one of them noticed the travellers. The monkey who was clearly their leader ordered that the men be detained. Since he wanted to know what the men thought of him, he commanded all rest of the monkeys to stand before him in a long line to his right and to his left, while a seat was prepared for him to sit on (this monkey had once seen the emperor, so he was ordering his monkeys to line up for him in the same way). The men were then told to come forward into the midst of the monkeys. The chief monkey said, 'Who am I?' The liar said, 'You are the emperor!' Then the monkey asked, 'And those whom you see standing before me: who are they?' The man answered, 'They are your noble companions, your chancellors, your officials and the commanders of your armies!' Because these lies flattered the monkey and his troops, he ordered that the man be showered with presents. All the monkeys were fooled by his flattery. Meanwhile, the man who always told the truth thought to himself, 'If that liar received such rewards for telling lies, then surely I will receive an even greater reward for telling the truth.' The chief monkey said to the second man, 'Now you tell me who I am, and who are these whom you see standing before me?' And the man who always loved the truth and never lied said to the monkey, 'You are simply a monkey, and all of these similar simians are monkeys as well!' The chief monkey immediately ordered the monkeys to attack the man with their teeth and claws because he had spoken the truth.
ground_truth_moral: Those who thrive on deception often retaliate against those who speak the truth.

summaries.direct_moral: Flattery can lead to downfall, while truthfulness invites harsh retribution.
summaries.cot_proverb: The truth is often met with hostility when it contradicts established power structures.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Truthfulness is often met with hostility when it contradicts established power structures.

fable_text: Some field-mice were playing in the woods where a lion was sleeping when one of the mice accidentally ran over the lion. The lion woke up and immediately grabbed the wretched little mouse with his paw. The mouse begged for mercy, since he had not meant to do the lion any harm. The lion decided that to kill such a tiny creature would be a cause for reproach rather than glory, so he forgave the mouse and let him go. A few days later, the lion fell into a pit and was trapped. He started to roar, and when the mouse heard him, he came running. Recognizing the lion in the trap, the mouse said to him, 'I have not forgotten the kindness that you showed me!' The mouse then began to gnaw at the cords binding the lion, cutting through the strands and undoing the clever ingenuity of the hunter's art. The mouse was thus able to restore the lion to the woods, setting him free from his captivity.
ground_truth_moral: No act of kindness, no matter how small, is ever wasted

summaries.direct_moral: Mercy shown in times of need can be repaid when it is truly needed.
summaries.cot_proverb: The greatest kindness shown in a moment can be repaid when it is needed most.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Mercy shown in a moment can be repaid by a timely act of reciprocal kindness.

fable_text: A herdsman tending his flock in a forest lost a Bull-calf from the fold. After a long and fruitless search, he made a vow that, if he could only discover the thief who had stolen the Calf, he would offer a lamb in sacrifice to Hermes, Pan, and the Guardian Deities of the forest. Not long afterwards, as he ascended a small hillock, he saw at its foot a Lion feeding on the Calf. Terrified at the sight, he lifted his eyes and his hands to heaven, and said: 'Just now I vowed to offer a lamb to the Guardian Deities of the forest if I could only find out who had robbed me; but now that I have discovered the thief, I would willingly add a full-grown Bull to the Calf I have lost, if I may only secure my own escape from him in safety.''
ground_truth_moral: Be careful what you wish for; your wish may be granted.

summaries.direct_moral: A desperate desire for personal safety can lead to a willingness to commit a greater transgression.
summaries.cot_proverb: Moral: When seeking justice, one often sacrifices the truth for a more convenient, albeit costly, resolution.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of justice or retribution can lead to a moral compromise where the desire for vengeance supersedes the original intent or the pursuit of a just outcome.

fable_text: A wolf, having stolen a lamb from a fold, was carrying him off to his lair. A Lion met him in the path, and seizing the lamb, took it from him. Standing at a safe distance, the Wolf exclaimed, 'You have unrighteously taken that which was mine from me!' To which the Lion jeeringly replied, 'It was righteously yours, eh? The gift of a friend?'
ground_truth_moral: Things you acquire through evil means can be taken from you by evil means.

summaries.direct_moral: Greed often leads to the betrayal of trust.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true measure of a relationship is often revealed by the context of the initial action.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Trust and ownership are often undermined by reciprocal claims of rightful possession.

fable_text: There is a tale of a lizard in a quest to match the length of a snake. In its effort to equal the snake's size, the lizard burst into pieces right down the middle.
ground_truth_moral: You will hurt yourself and accomplish nothing if you try to imitate someone who is far better than you are.

summaries.direct_moral: Hubris in striving for an impossible equality leads to self-destruction.
summaries.cot_proverb: When attempting to measure oneself against a vastly different standard, one risks being utterly destroyed in the process.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of an impossible equality leads to self-destruction.

fable_text: They say that in the beginning, when the animals were being formed, they received their endowments from Zeus. To some he gave strength, and to some speed, and to others wings. Man, however, was still naked so he said to Zeus, 'I am the only one that you have left without a gift.' Zeus replied, 'You are unaware of the gift you have obtained, but it is the greatest gift of all: you have received the gift of speech and the ability to reason, which has power both among the gods and among mortals; it is stronger than the strong and swifter than the swift.' Man then recognized the gift he had been given and bowed down before Zeus, offering him thanks.
ground_truth_moral: The greatest gifts are often overlooked in favor of envying others.

summaries.direct_moral: The greatest gift is the ability to reason.
summaries.cot_proverb: The gift of reason and speech is superior to physical attributes.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The value of a unique, inherent capacity is not measured by its immediate utility but by its profound transformative power.

fable_text: A hard-working widow woman had some maid servants whom she would rouse up for work at the sound of the cockcrow when it was still dark outside. The maids were burdened with endless tasks, so they decided it would be a good idea to kill the household rooster since it was the rooster who made their mistress get them up while it was still dark. Yet after they had killed the rooster, their desperate situation grew even worse: now that the mistress was no longer able to tell the hour by the rooster, she woke the maids up even earlier than before.
ground_truth_moral: People often make plans that turn out to be to their own disadvantage.

summaries.direct_moral: Desperate attempts to circumvent hardship often lead to unforeseen and even more detrimental consequences.
summaries.cot_proverb: Moral: Cutting off the cause of a problem often creates a worse one.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of a perceived solution leads to a compounding of problems, demonstrating that misguided efforts to solve a problem often create new, more severe ones.

fable_text: Some merchants were making a journey when they happened to meet a raven who was blind in one eye. The travellers halted and one of them said that the sign given to them by the raven meant that they should turn back home. Another member of the company protested, 'But how can such a bird predict what is going to happen to us, when he couldn't even predict the loss of his own eye in time to take preventive measures?'
ground_truth_moral: Someone who cannot manage his own affairs is not qualified to give advice to his neighbours.

summaries.direct_moral: Blind foresight is unreliable when the predictor is itself flawed.
summaries.cot_proverb: The inability of a flawed predictor to offer sound guidance.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Wisdom is not solely found in the ability to foresee future events when one is personally vulnerable.

fable_text: A snail found a mirror and when she saw how brightly he shone, she fell in love with him. She quickly climbed up onto the mirror's round surface and began to lick him. The snail clearly was no good for the mirror and only besmirched his lustrous radiance with filth and slime. A monkey then found the mirror after it had been dirtied by the snail, and remarked, 'That's what happens when you let someone like that walk all over you!'
ground_truth_moral: Marrying someone unworthy can lead to a tarnished reputation.

summaries.direct_moral: Excessive admiration can ruin what is precious.
summaries.cot_proverb: The more you try to possess or admire something beautiful, the more you risk damaging it through your own flawed desires.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Unchecked desire for superficial beauty invites self-destruction and invites exploitation by others.

fable_text: There was a miser who sold his property and bought a lump of gold. The man then buried his gold just outside the city walls, where he constantly went to visit and inspect it. One of the workmen noticed the man's behaviour and suspected the truth. Accordingly, after the man had gone away, he took the gold. When the man came back and found that the hiding place was empty, he began to cry and tear his hair. Someone saw the man's extravagant grief and asked him what was wrong. Then he said to the man, 'Enough of your grieving! Take a stone and put it where the gold was, and make believe the gold is still there: it's not as if you ever made any use of it!'
ground_truth_moral: The true value of money is not in its possession but in its use.

summaries.direct_moral: Excessive obsession with possessions leads to loss through misguided sentimentality.
summaries.cot_proverb: "Excessive care for something leads to its loss."
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Trusting suspicion leads to self-inflicted distress when one's greed blinds them to the truth.

fable_text: Moles are blind animals. A mole once said to his mother, 'I see a mulberry, mother!' Then he said, 'And I smell incense all around!' And he spoke a third time and said, 'And I can hear the sound of a tiny bronze ball.' His mother then said, 'My son, now I understand that in addition to lacking sight, you have also lost your sense of hearing and smell!'
ground_truth_moral: Boast of one thing and you will be found lacking in that and a few other things as well.

summaries.direct_moral: Overconfidence in multiple senses leads to a complete loss of all perception.
summaries.cot_proverb: The more a person focuses on a single deficiency, the more they neglect other aspects of their perception.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Perception is multifaceted and its complete understanding requires the integration of all sensory modalities.

fable_text: The mole is a handicapped animal: he is blind. There was once a mole who wanted to kiss his mother, but instead of pressing up against her mouth, he pressed against her private parts. His brothers realized what he was doing and one of them remarked, 'It serves you right! You had great expectations, but you have gone and lost even your sense of smell.'
ground_truth_moral: A sinful mind can even change a person's nature, causing it to be impaired.

summaries.direct_moral: Blind ambition can lead to a loss of true connection.
summaries.cot_proverb: The greater the expectation, the greater the fall.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Blind pursuit of desire leads to unintended and damaging consequences.

fable_text: The monkey gladly eats nuts because the nutmeats are tasty, but when she tastes the bitterness of the shell she throws the nut away and loses the nutmeat inside.
ground_truth_moral: Those who avoid hardship may miss out on valuable rewards.

summaries.direct_moral: True satisfaction is found only when the whole is appreciated, not just a part.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true value of something is often obscured by its exterior or initial appeal.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of immediate gratification blinds one to the potential for hidden harm or loss.

fable_text: The monkey gives birth to two babies, but after giving birth she does not mother them equally. She comforts one of them with cruel embraces, choking him with her unfortunate affection; meanwhile, she casts the other child away as superfluous and unimportant. This is the one who goes off into the wilds and is able to survive.
ground_truth_moral: The best intentions will not always ensure success.

summaries.direct_moral: Unequal love breeds disparity, while neglect can lead to survival.
summaries.cot_proverb: Moral: Unequal love breeds ruin.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Unbalanced affection leads to the neglect of the vulnerable.

fable_text: A boy who was carrying his teacher's writing tablet stole it and brought it triumphantly home to his mother who received the stolen goods with much delight. Next, the boy stole a piece of clothing, and by degrees he became a habitual criminal. As the boy grew older and became an adult, he stole items of greater and greater value. Time passed and the man was finally caught in the act and taken off to court where he was condemned to death: woe betide the trade of the thief! His mother stood behind him, weeping as she shouted, 'My son, what has become of you?' He said to his mother, 'Come closer, mother, and I will give you a final kiss.' She went up to him, and all of a sudden he bit her nose, tugging at it with his teeth until he cut it clean off. Then he said to her, 'Mother, if only you had beaten me at the very beginning when I brought you the writing tablet, then I would not have been condemned to death!'
ground_truth_moral: Tear out vice by the roots and the branches will wither away.

summaries.direct_moral: Greed and disregard for trust ultimately lead to ruin.
summaries.cot_proverb: The seed of transgression, when nurtured by lack of restraint, inevitably brings about a downfall, and the guilty often seeks to shift blame for their ruin.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Unchecked transgression leads to escalating harm and ultimate self-destruction.

fable_text: A snake and a weasel were fighting with one another inside a house. The mice who lived in the house were always being hunted down by either the snake or the weasel, so when they saw the snake and the weasel fighting with each other, they emerged from their holes and scurried around. When the weasel and the snake saw what was happening, they stopped fighting with each other and turned their attention to the mice.
ground_truth_moral: In conflicts between powerful parties, bystanders may suffer unintended consequences.

summaries.direct_moral: Conflict between powerful entities distracts the vulnerable, allowing a greater threat to exploit the opportunity.
summaries.cot_proverb: When two adversaries distract each other, the vulnerable suffer the most.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Self-interest and rivalry can impede the recognition of a more pressing collective concern.

fable_text: A weasel, enfeebled by old age and senility, was no longer able to pursue the swift-footed mice, so she decided to coat herself with flour and lie down nonchalantly in a dark corner of the house. One of the mice thought that she must be something good to eat, but as soon as he pounced, the weasel caught him and consigned him to oblivion; another mouse did the same, and a third mouse likewise met his doom. A few mice later, another mouse arrived: his skin was wrinkled with extreme old age and he had escaped many a time from snares and traps. Already at a distance he recognized the ambush prepared by their cunning enemy. 'You there, lying in the corner,' said the mouse, 'I wish you well if -- and only if -- you really are made of flour!'
ground_truth_moral: Appearances can be deceiving.

summaries.direct_moral: Blind reliance on false appearances invites destruction.
summaries.cot_proverb: The appearance of weakness invites exploitation, and the true nature of a creature is revealed by its actions rather than its facade.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The danger of misplaced trust and assumption based on superficial appearance leads to inevitable harm, even when the apparent victim is innocent.

fable_text: A robber had murdered someone along the road. When the bystanders began to chase him, he dropped the bloody corpse and ran away. Some travellers coming from the opposite direction asked the man how he had stained his hands. The man said that he had just climbed down from a mulberry tree, but as he was speaking, his pursuers caught up with him. They seized the murderer and crucified him on a mulberry tree. The tree said to him, 'It does not trouble me at all to assist in your execution, since you tried to smear me with the murder that you yourself committed!'
ground_truth_moral: If often happens that even honest people do not hesitate to persecute someone who has slandered them.

summaries.direct_moral: Guilt cannot save a wrongdoer from ultimate reckoning.
summaries.cot_proverb: Moral: Haste makes waste.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Guilt cannot absolve the inherent consequence of one's own transgression.

fable_text: Two mules were walking along, weighed down by two different loads: one mule was laden with bags full of money, while the other mule was carrying sacks stuffed with barley. The mule who was flush with money proudly lifted his head, tossing the little bell on his neck back and forth, while his companion simply followed along at a calm and quiet pace. All of a sudden, the mules were set upon by robbers who attacked them from an ambush. In the violent struggle, the rich mule was slashed with a sword and the robbers stole his money, yet they paid no attention at all to the worthless barley. When the mule who had lost all his riches began to complain, the other mule declared, 'As for me, I am quite content to have been treated with contempt: it means that I suffered no injury and lost none of my possessions!'
ground_truth_moral: There is little risk in being poor, while being rich can get you into trouble.

summaries.direct_moral: True contentment lies in enduring hardship without loss.
summaries.cot_proverb: The measure of worth is not in material wealth but in the preservation of what is essential.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: True contentment lies in valuing what is essential over superficial wealth.

fable_text: There was once a musician who had no talent whatsoever but he played his lyre in a room that had thick plaster upon the walls so when he heard the echoing sound, he concluded that he must be an excellent musician indeed. Puffed up with pride, he decided to perform on the stage. But when he made his debut at the theatre, his performance was so dreadful that the audience threw stones at him, driving him off the stage.
ground_truth_moral: There's no reading of ourselves but with other mans eyes.

summaries.direct_moral: False self-perception leads to public failure.
summaries.cot_proverb: The more a person praises themselves, the more they are exposed to harsh reality.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Self-deception based on perception rather than reality leads to disastrous consequences.

fable_text: A swallow once asked a nightingale why she didn't want to come and take up residence with her. The nightingale wept and cried, 'No, it is impossible for me to do that. It brings to mind the grief suffered by my ancestors, which is why I dwell in the wilderness.'
ground_truth_moral: When people feel grief for their misfortunes, they avoid the place where that grief overtook them.

summaries.direct_moral: Grief and past suffering can create barriers to connection and companionship.
summaries.cot_proverb: "The grief of the past keeps one from embracing the present."
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The depth of past suffering prevents present connection and vulnerability.

fable_text: The Sun and the North Wind were quarrelling with each other as to which of the two of them would be able to make a man disrobe. The North Wind went first, blowing fiercely against the man. Yet as the man grew colder and colder, he only wrapped himself up more snugly in his cloak, clutching at it tightly so as to keep a firm grip no matter how hard the wind might be blowing. Thus the North Wind did the man no harm at all and failed to make him strip off his clothes. Next, the Sun began to shine upon the man so brightly that the very air of the day grew hotter and hotter. The man immediately took off his cloak and bundled it up on his shoulders.
ground_truth_moral: Persuasion is better than Force.

summaries.direct_moral: True strength lies in inner resolve, whether against harsh external forces or overwhelming brilliance.
summaries.cot_proverb: The fiercest force, when met with inner resolve, proves ineffective against a determined will.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Adaptation to opposing forces reveals that true resilience lies in internal fortitude rather than external struggle.

fable_text: An old woman saw a wine jar that had been drained empty, but the lees of the exquisite wine still sent forth a pleasant odour from the noble vessel. The woman greedily imbibed the smell, deeply inhaling through both nostrils, and said, 'Oh sweet spirits, I do declare, how excellent you must once have been to have left behind such fine remains!'
ground_truth_moral: The memory of a good deed lives.

summaries.direct_moral: Greedily appreciating the remnants of something past prevents one from valuing the present.
summaries.cot_proverb: The remnants of greatness can still offer a pleasing illusion of past splendor.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Appreciating the remnants of past excellence can lead to misplaced admiration and a failure to recognize the present reality.

fable_text: A young man was walking along on a blazing hot day when he met an old woman who was going the same way. Seeing that she was dreadfully exhausted from the heat of the day and the demands of the journey, he felt sorry for her weakness and when the woman simply didn't have the strength to go any further, he picked her up off the ground and carried her on his shoulders. While he was carrying her this way, the young man was so strongly aroused by shameful thoughts that he had an erection. Spurred by wanton lust and hot desire, he immediately put the old woman down on the ground and had sex with her. Being simple-minded, the woman asked him, 'What are you doing to me?' He answered, 'You are too heavy to carry, so I've decided to carve off some of your flesh.' The man satisfied himself and then picked the woman up off the ground again and set her on his shoulders. After he had gone some way down the road, the old woman said to him, 'If I am still too heavy a burden for you, you can put me down again and carve off some more of me!'
ground_truth_moral: People often disguise their selfish desires as practical needs to justify their actions.

summaries.direct_moral: Compassion can mask lust, leading to exploitation and further abuse.
summaries.cot_proverb: The desire for gain outweighs mercy.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Unchecked desire corrupts compassion into exploitation.

fable_text: War broke out between all the beasts and the birds. When the ostrich was captured, she fooled both sides by being both a bird and a beast: she showed the birds her head, and the beasts her feet.
ground_truth_moral: Half-truths in some cases, are foul, and dishonest; in others, laudable; and in some again, not only honest, but necessary.

summaries.direct_moral: True cunning can deceive even the fiercest adversaries.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true nature of a person can be deceptive when they play to the biases of others.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Adaptability in deception can circumvent rigid divisions of conflict.

fable_text: A panther foolishly happened to have fallen into a pit. The local villagers saw her there and some of them attacked her with sticks or pelted her with stones. There were others who felt sorry for the creature since she seemed sure to die even though she had not done any harm, so they brought her bread to keep up her strength. Night fell and everyone went home, confident that they would find the panther dead when the next day dawned. However, as soon as she recovered from her weakness and regained her strength, the panther escaped from the pit with a mighty leap and hurried quickly home to her den. A few days later she descended upon the village, slaughtering the sheep and even killing the shepherds as she laid waste to everything around her in a furious attack of rage. At this point even the people who had shown mercy to the beast began to fear what lay in store for them. Without a word about the damage that the panther had wrought, they begged her just to spare their lives. The panther then said, 'I am well aware of who pelted me with stones and who gave me bread, so put aside your fears. I have returned as an enemy only to those who wanted to hurt me.'
ground_truth_moral: People who have been treated with contempt repay the deed in kind.

summaries.direct_moral: Mercy shown to a creature can invite greater retribution when that creature is capable of fierce defense.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true nature of a creature is revealed not by its initial misfortune or the kindness shown to it, but by its inherent capacity for retribution when provoked.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Mercy shown in times of apparent vulnerability can invite future retribution when the wronged party regains power.

fable_text: A man who kept roosters also bought himself a partridge and let it loose to live among his other birds. When the roosters pecked at the partridge and chased him away, the partridge became very dejected, thinking that the roosters were attacking him because he was an outsider. After a little while, however, the partridge saw the roosters fighting and attacking one another. The partridge then put aside his grief and said, 'Well, I am certainly not going to grieve over this any longer, now that I see the way they fight with one another.
ground_truth_moral: A wise person readily tolerates the insolence of strangers when he sees those same strangers mistreating one another.

summaries.direct_moral: True understanding comes from observing the conflicts of others rather than focusing on personal victimization.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true measure of conflict is not in isolation, but in observing the nature of the conflict itself.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Empathy grows from observing the struggles of others.

fable_text: A woman had lost her beloved husband of many years and had laid his body in the ground. It was impossible to tear her away from his grave, and she filled her days with weeping. Everyone repeated glowingly that this woman was an example of a truly faithful wife. Meanwhile, some men who had pillaged the temple of Jupiter were condemned to death for their crime against the god. After they had been crucified, soldiers were stationed by the crosses so that the families of the executed criminals could not recover their bodies. This all took place next to the tomb where the woman had secluded herself. One of the guards happened to be thirsty and asked the woman's maidservant to bring him some water in the middle of the night. As it happened, the maid had been helping her mistress prepare for bed, as the widow had maintained her vigil long into the night and was still sitting up by the light of the lamp. The door was open just a crack and when the soldier peeped inside, he saw a woman of exceptional beauty. He was immediately enthralled and inflamed with lust, and an irresistible desire began gradually to well up inside him. His crafty ingenuity found a thousand reasons to see the widow again and again. Acquiescing to this regular daily contact, the widow slowly but surely became more and more inclined towards her guest, and soon an even closer bond united her heart to his. While the guard was spending his nights in the widow's embrace, one of the corpses was spirited away from the cross. The soldier was upset and told the woman what had happened. That exemplary woman said, 'Don't worry!' and with these words, she handed over her husband's corpse to be nailed to the cross, so that the soldier would not be punished for dereliction of duty.
ground_truth_moral: Temptation can lead even the most virtuous individuals to compromise their integrity and values.

summaries.direct_moral: True devotion can be exploited by lust, leading to a tragic compromise of duty.
summaries.cot_proverb: The temptation of desire can corrupt even the most virtuous, and the pursuit of illicit gratification can lead to moral compromise.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of personal desire can corrupt even the most virtuous individuals, leading to moral compromise for self-interest.

fable_text: Some woodcutters splitting a wild pine tree drove wedges into the trunk, prying it apart and thus making their work easier. The pine tree groaned and said, 'I cannot blame the axe, who had no connection with my root, but these utterly despicable wedges are my own children. Pounded into me this way and that, they are going to tear me apart!'
ground_truth_moral: The bad things that strangers do to you are never as terrible as the things done to you by the members of your own family.

summaries.direct_moral: Brute force used without understanding can cause further destruction.
summaries.cot_proverb: The moral is that brute force applied without understanding or respect will only result in further destruction.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The destructive means employed to achieve a goal ultimately harm the source of the work itself, demonstrating that the tools or methods used in pursuit of a desired outcome can inflict unintended and damaging consequences upon the very thing they seek to manipulate or utilize.

fable_text: They say that swans sing when they are about to die. A certain man chanced upon a swan that was for sale and bought him, since he had heard that swans sing very beautifully. At the man's next dinner party, he came and got the swan, expecting that the bird would serenade his guests at dinner. The swan, however, was completely silent. Later on, when the swan realized that he was about to die, he began to sing his funeral dirge. When his owner heard him, he said, 'Well, if you are going to sing this song only at the moment of your death, then I was a fool for having commanded you to do it. I should have ordered you to be butchered instead!'
ground_truth_moral: Some people will agree to do things under compulsion that they are not willing to do as a favour.

summaries.direct_moral: Expecting a performance at death, the man regretted commanding the swan to sing only at the moment of its demise.
summaries.cot_proverb: The expectation of beauty or performance is misplaced when the true nature of the subject is considered.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The expectation of a specific performance at a critical moment leads to a failure of the intended outcome, revealing the folly of relying on external signs for genuine value or fulfillment.

fable_text: A wolf followed along after a flock of sheep without doing them any harm. At first the shepherd kept his eye on the wolf as a potential enemy to the flock and never let him out of his sight. But as the wolf continued to accompany the shepherd and did not make any kind of attempt to raid the flock, the shepherd eventually began to regard the wolf more as a guardian of the flock than as a threat. Then, when the shepherd happened to have to go to town, he commended the sheep to the wolf in his absence. The wolf seized his chance and attacked the sheep, slaughtering most of the flock. When the shepherd came back and saw that his flock had been utterly destroyed, he said, 'It serves me right! How could I have ever trusted my sheep to a wolf?'
ground_truth_moral: One should never trust those with a history of deceit.

summaries.direct_moral: Blind trust in a supposed ally can lead to devastating consequences.
summaries.cot_proverb: Trusting a potential threat too closely can lead to a greater loss when the opportunity arises.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Unchecked trust can lead to vulnerability when a perceived protector exploits a moment of absence.

fable_text: After her months of pregnancy had passed, a woman about to deliver her child was lying on the ground, moaning and weeping. Her husband urged her to rest her body on the bed, so that she might better relieve herself of nature's burden, but the woman refused and said, 'I hardly think that my trouble could come to an end in the very place where it was first conceived!'
ground_truth_moral: No one gladly revisits a place where they got hurt.

summaries.direct_moral: Do not expect a difficult situation to resolve in the same manner it began.
summaries.cot_proverb: The trouble of childbirth is not necessarily resolved by returning to the source of the difficulty.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The insistence on enduring suffering rather than seeking relief reveals a stubborn attachment to a difficult state, even when that state is clearly causing distress.

fable_text: A wolf once saw a raven sitting on a ram. The wolf sighed deeply and said, 'That raven is a happy fellow, born under a lucky star! Wherever he sits, whatever he says, whatever he does, nobody criticizes him in any way. But if I were to clamber up on a ram like that, anyone who saw me would start shouting and hurry to chase me away -- as if they had the ram's best interests at heart!'
ground_truth_moral: The unjust man is envious of anyone else's good fortune.

summaries.direct_moral: Unjustified envy breeds only self-criticism.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true measure of a person's happiness or freedom is not in their inherent virtue, but in the protection afforded to them by their circumstances.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: True happiness or undeserved fortune invites unwarranted scrutiny and hostility, whereas undeserved misfortune is often met with silent acceptance.

fable_text: A raven who was looking for food noticed a snake stretched out asleep in the sun. The raven jumped on the snake and grabbed it, but the snake then twisted back around and bit him. As he was dying, the raven said, 'What a fool I was! The windfall I found has turned out to be fatal!'
ground_truth_moral: Desire things you don't understand and you accept the consequences.

summaries.direct_moral: Greed or rash action can lead to self-destruction.
summaries.cot_proverb: The fool finds a trap for himself.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Greed or rash action, when misguided, can lead to self-destruction.

fable_text: There was once a stork who quarrelled with his wife and poked out her eye with his beak. The stork was ashamed for having inflicted such an injury on his wife so he flew away to go live somewhere else. A raven ran into the stork and asked the reason for his journey.
