summaries.conceptual_abstract: The necessity of yielding to a subordinate's insistence, even when illogical, leads to a shared, albeit costly, resolution.

fable_text: A shepherd penning his sheep in the fold for the night was about to shut up a wolf with them, when his Dog perceiving the wolf said, 'Master, how can you expect the sheep to be safe if you admit a wolf into the fold?'
ground_truth_moral: Be watchful or all will be lost.

summaries.direct_moral: Do not invite danger into a situation where safety is at stake.
summaries.cot_proverb: When in doubt, keep out the danger.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Prudence dictates that one should not invite danger into a situation intended for protection.

fable_text: Zeus once ordered Prometheus to show mankind the two ways: one the way of freedom and the other the way of slavery. Prometheus made the way of freedom rough at the beginning, impassable and steep, with no water anywhere to drink, full of brambles, and beset with dangers on all sides at first. Eventually, however, it became a smooth plain, lined with paths and filled with groves of fruit trees and waterways. Thus the distressing experience ended in repose for those who breath the air of freedom. The way of slavery, however, started out as a smooth plain at the beginning, full of flowers, pleasant to look at and quite luxurious, but in the end it became impassable, steep and insurmountable on all sides.
ground_truth_moral: It is better to endure short-term struggles for long-term benefits than the other way around.

summaries.direct_moral: The path to freedom often involves initial hardship for eventual ease, while the path of servitude may appear pleasant but ultimately prove inescapable.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true nature of freedom is often arduous and challenging in its pursuit, while the illusion of ease can mask a deeper, inescapable bondage.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The initial difficulty of achieving freedom is necessary for its eventual benefit, while superficial ease can mask profound entrapment.

fable_text: A Frog and a Mouse, who had long been rivals for the sovereignty of a certain marsh, and had many a skirmish and running fight together, agreed one day to settle the matter, once for all, by a fair and open combat. They met, and each, armed with the point of a bulrush for a spear, was ready, if need be, to fight to the death. The fight began in earnest, and there is no knowing how it might have ended, had not a Kite, seeing them from afar, pounced down and carried off both heroes in her talons.
ground_truth_moral: When fools fight others run away with the prize.

summaries.direct_moral: Hubris invites disaster when rivals seek to settle disputes through direct conflict.
summaries.cot_proverb: The surest way to resolve a conflict is to seek a direct confrontation, but external forces can often resolve disputes without direct engagement.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of absolute resolution through direct confrontation often leads to unforeseen and destructive consequences when external, superior forces are present.

fable_text: When a fisherman happened to see an octopus during the winter, he said, 'If I take off my clothes and jump in after him, I will freeze! But if I do not catch that octopus, I condemn my children to die of starvation!'
ground_truth_moral: There is no easy choice during desperate times.

summaries.direct_moral: Fear of risk can lead to a worse outcome than embracing necessary danger.
summaries.cot_proverb: The moral is: Hesitation in the face of risk can lead to greater loss.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Prudence demands a careful balance between risk and necessity.

fable_text: A certain man was sitting on the beach counting the waves as they broke against the shore. When he lost count of the waves he got angry and frustrated. A sly fox then approached the man and said, 'Good sir, what is the point of getting angry about the waves that have already gone by? You need to just put them out of your mind and pick up counting again where you left off!'
ground_truth_moral: Do not dwell on what has passed.

summaries.direct_moral: Do not waste energy on what is past; focus instead on the present task.
summaries.cot_proverb: The past cannot be regretted; focus on the present.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The futility of dwelling on past failures or disturbances.

fable_text: The clay which Prometheus used when he fashioned man was not mixed with water but with tears. Therefore, one should not try to dispense entirely with tears, since they are inevitable.
ground_truth_moral: Sadness is a part of life.

summaries.direct_moral: One should not attempt to eliminate tears, for they are inevitable.
summaries.cot_proverb: The tears are inevitable.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Tears are an unavoidable part of human experience and should not be entirely eliminated.

fable_text: An owl, in her wisdom, counseled the Birds that when the acorn first began to sprout, to pull it all up out of the ground and not allow it to grow. She said acorns would produce mistletoe, from which an irremediable poison, the bird-lime, would be extracted and by which they would be captured. The Owl next advised them to pluck up the seed of the flax, which men had sown, as it was a plant which boded no good to them. And, lastly, the Owl, seeing an archer approach, predicted that this man, being on foot, would contrive darts armed with feathers which would fly faster than the wings of the Birds themselves. The Birds gave no credence to these warning words, but considered the Owl to be beside herself and said that she was mad. But afterwards, finding her words were true, they wondered at her knowledge and deemed her to be the wisest of birds. Hence it is that when she appears they look to her as knowing all things, while she no longer gives them advice, but in solitude laments their past folly.
ground_truth_moral: Destroy the seed of evil, or it will grow up to your ruin.

summaries.direct_moral: Hasty action based on flawed understanding leads to ruin.
summaries.cot_proverb: The folly of ignoring wise counsel leads to regret when the consequences are realized.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Wisdom is often disregarded until its truth is proven, at which point it is revered as infallible.

fable_text: There was a woman of Sybaris who broke a jar. The jar then got someone to testify as a witness, but the woman of Sybaris said, 'I swear by the Maiden, you would have been far wiser if you'd gone right away to get a bandage, instead of making a court-case out of it.'
ground_truth_moral: Action is more valuable than arguments.

summaries.direct_moral: Haste in dispute often proves less valuable than timely, practical action.
summaries.cot_proverb: The folly of pursuing legalistic detail over practical care.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Wisdom in the face of a minor incident is more valuable than the pursuit of legalistic vindication.

fable_text: The animals were Orpheus' companions, and while they enjoyed and admired his music, they never attempted to imitate it. But a few of the dogs, those shameless and meddlesome creatures, set out to make some music of their own. They straightaway went off by themselves to practice, changing themselves into human form as they continued their musical pursuits. This is where lyre players come from, and even today they cannot completely overcome their inborn nature. As a result, they preserve the teachings of Orpheus, but only to a small degree; for the most part it is the canine music that endures.
ground_truth_moral: Nature reveals itself.

summaries.direct_moral: Innate nature often surpasses imitation.
summaries.cot_proverb: The imitation of genius is often superficial compared to innate, unrefined expression.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Innate tendencies often find a way to express themselves despite the influence of established excellence.

fable_text: In the wintertime, a Dog curled up in as small a space as possible on account of the cold, determined to make himself a house. However when the summer returned again, he lay asleep stretched at his full length and appeared to himself to be of a great size. Now he considered that it would be neither an easy nor a necessary work to make himself such a house as would accommodate him.
ground_truth_moral: It's easy to put off today what can be done tomorrow.

summaries.direct_moral: True comfort is not found in extremes, for what seems necessary in one season may be inconvenient in another.
summaries.cot_proverb: Summer's ease breeds complacency, while hardship fosters foresight.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Adaptation to changing circumstances dictates a flexible approach to necessity.

fable_text: At one time, it seemed to all the beasts that they should hold a meeting and assembly, as there were frequent complaints among them about one another. When opinions were exchanged back and forth, the hares are said to have addressed the assembly: they believed it fair that all beasts should have the same dignity and equal power, and that it should not be the case that some are much stronger while others are weaker; for in this way, the inferior would be subject to the superior for plunder and prey. The Lions made this reply: 'Your words, O Hares! are good; but they lack both claws and teeth such as we have.'
ground_truth_moral: Be prepared to defend your position.

summaries.direct_moral: True strength is demonstrated by capability, not by mere agreement on equality.
summaries.cot_proverb: The strength of the powerful is inherently superior to the claims of the weak.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Equality of status does not negate the inherent differences in capability.

fable_text: An omnivorous, gluttonous mouse was creeping through the house when he noticed an oyster with its mouth wide open, so he bit into the false flesh of the oyster's wet beard. Suddenly the door to the oyster's house slammed shut in a painful clasp. The mouse was enclosed in a prison with no hope of escape: he himself had closed the door to the tomb of his own death.
ground_truth_moral: Greed often overreaches itself.

summaries.direct_moral: Gluttony leads to self-inflicted ruin.
summaries.cot_proverb: The fool's attempt to satisfy a greedy desire leads to a self-imposed trap.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Self-inflicted peril stems from unchecked desire.

fable_text: Aesop said that the eyes were distressed because they considered themselves the most worthy part of the body, yet they saw the mouth enjoying every possible pleasure, especially honey, the sweetest substance of all. Filled with resentment, the eyes complained to the man. But when the man put honey in his eyes, they felt a stinging pain and burst into tears, since they thought that the honey was harsh and unpleasant.
ground_truth_moral: Be careful what you wish for.

summaries.direct_moral: Those who resent others' joys often find their own pleasures to be painful.
summaries.cot_proverb: The eye that judges others' pleasures finds its own joy diminished by its own narrow standards.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of self-importance leads to a failure to appreciate genuine joy or value in others, resulting in a distorted perception of pleasure.

fable_text: Aesop, that wise man from Phrygia, once saw a winning athlete who was making extravagant boasts, so he asked whether the man's opponent had been more powerfully built. The athlete replied, 'Watch what you're saying! I was much stronger than he was.' 'You fool!' said Aesop. 'What kind of prize do you deserve if you were the stronger athlete and simply triumphed over an inferior opponent? You might have earned my grudging admiration if your opponent had in fact been superior to you in strength, so that you had to defeat him by means of your exceptional athletic prowess.'
ground_truth_moral: Degrading your opponents only diminishes your own victory.

summaries.direct_moral: True victory lies in overcoming a worthy adversary, not merely boasting against the weak.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true measure of achievement lies not in defeating an inferior foe, but in overcoming a worthy challenge.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The true measure of achievement lies not in victory over an inferior rival, but in overcoming a worthy challenge.

fable_text: A troublesome crow seated herself on the back of a Sheep. The Sheep, much against his will, carried her backward and forward for a long time, and at last said, 'If you had treated a dog in this way, you would have had your deserts from his sharp teeth.' To this the Crow replied, 'I despise the weak and yield to the strong. I know whom I may bully and whom I must flatter; and I thus prolong my life to a good old age.'
ground_truth_moral: Pick your enemies.

summaries.direct_moral: Despise the weak, for the strong will ultimately prevail.
summaries.cot_proverb: The stronger individual exploits the weaker one for personal gain, and the weaker individual seeks advantage through manipulation.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The principle of reciprocal exploitation demonstrates that power dynamics are inherently self-perpetuating and destructive.

fable_text: There is a bird that country folk call 'ground-bird,' which makes sense, because this bird makes her nest on the ground. A ground-bird happened to run into a conniving vixen, and as soon as she noticed her, the ground-bird flew even higher on her wings. 'Greetings!' said the vixen, 'May I ask why you are running away? It's not as if there were not plenty of food for me here in the field -- grasshoppers, beetles, locusts in abundance -- so there's nothing for you to be afraid of. I am actually very fond of you because of your retiring manner and honest ways.' The singing bird replied, 'Your words are certainly pleasant to hear, but I am no equal to you on the ground. Here in the air, though, I can hold my own. Why don't you come on up with me? This is the place where I would trust you with my life!'
ground_truth_moral: Flatters are not to be trusted.

summaries.direct_moral: True worth is found not in superficial abundance but in the strength of one's own domain.
summaries.cot_proverb: Moral: Do not equate your strength with another's perceived abundance.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Trust is conditional upon perceived strength and the ability to offer a superior exchange.

fable_text: A Boy sat weeping upon the side of a well. A Thief happening to come by just at the same time, asked him why he wept. The Boy, sighing and sobbing, showed a bit of cord, and said that a silver tankard had come off from it, and was now at the bottom of the well. The Thief pulled off his clothes and went down into the well, meaning to keep the tankard for himself. Having groped about for some time without finding it, he came up again, and found not only the Boy gone, but his 0wn clothes also, the dissembling rogue having made off with them.
ground_truth_moral: Every tale is not to be believed.

summaries.direct_moral: Greed often leads to the loss of what is desired.
summaries.cot_proverb: Moral: Greed often leads to the loss of what is sought.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Greed often leads to the loss of what is desired.

fable_text: Carried from his fresh-water pond by a rushing stream, a fresh-water fish rushed headlong into the waters of the sea. The shameless creature scorned the scaly schools in the sea and boasted endlessly of his own illustrious origins. A salt-water fish could not suffer this refugee in his own ancestral waters and he spoke these pungent words, naturally salted with wit: 'Enough of your empty lies and pretentious talk; you yourself are the best evidence of their absurdity. If the two of us are captured and hauled in by the same dripping net, I will be able to prove to you just who is more highly regarded by the crowd of onlookers: you will see that I am bought by the connoisseur at a very high price indeed, while you will be sold to an undiscriminating commoner for a mere penny or two!'
ground_truth_moral: Value is what others think of you, not what you think of yourself.

summaries.direct_moral: Boasting about one's origins is futile when faced with true experience.
summaries.cot_proverb: Moral: The boast of origin often reveals the true measure of one's worth.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Hubris leads to self-destruction when arrogance blinds one to the true measure of their worth.

fable_text: A countryman who needed to cross a river tested it by sounding it up and down to find the best place to ford. In his testing, he made this observation: where the water flowed smoothly, he found it to be the deepest; conversely, where it was shallow, it was where the water made the most noise.
ground_truth_moral: There is danger in the silent enemy.

summaries.direct_moral: True depth is often found where the surface appears least turbulent.
summaries.cot_proverb: The measure of a situation by its surface appearance rather than its true depth.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of a desired outcome leads to a flawed understanding of reality, where the most apparent or easily accessible state is mistaken for the most profound or valuable.

fable_text: A Mouse that was raised in a Chest, living all her days on what the Mistress of the House stored within, happened one day to tumble out over the edge and stumbled upon a very tasty morsel while searching for a way back inside. No sooner had she tasted it than she exclaimed how foolish she had been to believe that true happiness could only be found within that box.
ground_truth_moral: We should not love our homeland so much that we ignore the possibility of finding greater happiness elsewhere.

summaries.direct_moral: True happiness is not found in confinement, but in the experience of the world.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true measure of happiness is not found in possessions or confines, but in the experience itself.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: True fulfillment is not found in external confines but in the realization of one's own limitations.

fable_text: A certain farmer complained that the beards of his corn sometimes cut the reapers' and threshers' fingers, and so he asked Ceres to make his corn grow without beards. His request was granted, and the little birds ate up all his grain. 'Fool that I was,' he said, 'to lose the support of my life rather than risk the pricking of my fingers.'
ground_truth_moral: A small inconvenience is compensated for by a greater usefulness.

summaries.direct_moral: Self-preservation can lead to greater loss than accepting minor inconvenience.
summaries.cot_proverb: The cost of avoiding minor inconvenience can lead to greater disaster.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Self-preservation can lead to greater ruin.

fable_text: A Country Fellow had the luck to catch a Hawk chasing a Pigeon. The Hawk begged for her life, saying she never harmed the Country-man, so she hoped he wouldn't harm her. The Country-man replied, 'And what harm did the Pigeon ever do to you?' By your own reasoning, you should expect to be treated the same way you would have treated this Pigeon.
ground_truth_moral: It's wise to think before we speak.

summaries.direct_moral: Do not judge others based on their past actions without considering the context of the situation.
summaries.cot_proverb: When one seeks to excuse oneself by pointing to another's past actions, one risks being judged by the same standard.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Reciprocity demands a balanced consideration of harm and innocence.

fable_text: A Spider saw a Swallow catching Flies and quickly started making a Web to catch Swallows, thinking it was an invasion of her territory. But the Birds easily broke through the Web and flew away with it. Well, said the Spider, I see that catching Birds is not my skill; and so she went back to her usual work of catching Flies.
ground_truth_moral: A wise person will not attempt anything without having the right means to achieve it.

summaries.direct_moral: Aggression against a superior skill will ultimately fail.
summaries.cot_proverb: The skill of one is not the skill of another, and envy of another's success leads to failure.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The assertion of superiority leads to the failure of an ill-conceived strategy when confronted by superior skill.

fable_text: A Magpie asked a Pigeon why she kept nesting in the same place when her chicks were always taken before they could fly. 'That's my simplicity,' said the Pigeon. 'I mean no harm, and I suspect none.'
ground_truth_moral: Trusting as you wish to be trusted doesn't work between a rogue and an honest person.

summaries.direct_moral: Simplicity can sometimes lead to vulnerability.
summaries.cot_proverb: Simplicity often invites exploitation.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The inherent vulnerability of innocence or simplicity can lead to a tragic outcome when faced with the indifference or harsh reality of the world.

fable_text: By the beak and claws of a Cuckoo, one might mistake her for a kind of Hawk, except one eats worms and the other eats meat. One day, a Hawk mocked a Cuckoo for her simple diet. 'If you want to look like a Hawk, why don't you live like one?' The Cuckoo felt a bit offended, but later, as she passed a pigeon house, she saw the skin of that very Hawk on a pole atop the dove house. 'Well!' said the Cuckoo, 'Wouldn't it have been better to eat worms than pigeons?'
ground_truth_moral: Pride and vanity leads to our downfall.

summaries.direct_moral: Appearances can be deceiving, and one should value what is truly suited to oneself.
summaries.cot_proverb: The appearance of a creature does not determine its true nature or value.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: True understanding requires recognizing the inherent value and suitability of one's own nature rather than judging others based on superficial appearances.

fable_text: A fox was walking by a certain farm and saw a crowd of women who, in total silence, were eating many sumptuously roasted chickens. The fox turned to the women and said, 'What shouts there would be against me, and what howling of dogs, if I were to do what you are doing!' A certain old woman said, 'Most wretched creature! We are eating those things which are ours; you take your pleasure in things that belong to others.'
ground_truth_moral: We should not to conclude that we are allowed to do to other people's things what is permitted to them in their own houses.

summaries.direct_moral: Covetousness leads to the loss of what is rightfully yours.
summaries.cot_proverb: When others enjoy what is rightfully yours, do not seek to take it by force.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Possession breeds envy, and the desire for what is not one's is often met with condemnation.

fable_text: There were many plump chickens together in a coop, some very fair and fat, while others did not grow well despite feeding. The fat ones would often make fun of the lean ones, calling them starvelings, until one day the cook was ordered to prepare some chickens for supper and to be sure to take the best from the pen. When that time came, those with the most flesh wished they had less, and it would have been better for them.
ground_truth_moral: The life of the poor is often safer than the life of the rich.

summaries.direct_moral: Greed and envy will cause those who have abundance to diminish themselves for the sake of perceived superiority.
summaries.cot_proverb: The greedy exploit the misfortune of others for their own gain.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Self-interest leads to the exploitation of the vulnerable.

fable_text: The Tree was complaining about the Oxen's lack of gratitude. 'How often,' said the Tree, 'have I fed you with my leaves and sheltered you with my shade? And now you drag me over dirt and stones like this!' 'Alas!' cried the Oxen. 'Can't you see how we pant and groan, and how we are forced to do this?' The Tree realized they were doing it unwillingly and forgave them.
ground_truth_moral: What we are forced to do by a greater power and necessity is not truly our own action.

summaries.direct_moral: Unwilling service can be forgiven when the true nature of hardship is recognized.
summaries.cot_proverb: The ungrateful do not appreciate what is freely given.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Forgiveness is necessary when the burdens of obligation cause one party to feel aggrieved, and understanding the other's suffering can lead to reconciliation.

fable_text: There was a fine grove of trees, all tall, fair, and smooth, except for one small tree that was twisted and bent, and the others mocked it. The owner of the woods needed to build a house and told his workers to take timber from the grove, cutting down every tree that was suitable. They did as they were told, and this ugly little tree was left standing.
ground_truth_moral: Famous beauties are rarely lucky.

summaries.direct_moral: Difference in form does not negate worth.
summaries.cot_proverb: The flawed are often overlooked when others seek perfection.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of conformity leads to the marginalization of difference.

fable_text: A Stork, who was there when a Swan was singing her final song, told her it was unusual to sing so much at this time. He asked her why she was doing it. The Swan replied, 'I am about to enter a place where I will no longer face traps, guns, or hunger. Who wouldn't be happy about such freedom?'
ground_truth_moral: Do not fear death, as it is the final goodbye to all the troubles, pains, and dangers of life.

summaries.direct_moral: True happiness lies in the anticipation of freedom from hardship.
summaries.cot_proverb: The greatest joy is found in the anticipation of freedom.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Courage is sometimes motivated by the desire for liberation from present suffering.

fable_text: A simple girl was wringing her hands and almost crying herself to death. And what was the matter? She had just parted ways with her sweetheart. 'Oh, you fool,' said one of her neighbors, 'to torment yourself over such a fellow!' 'No,' said the girl, 'I'm not so troubled about losing the man, but he took his coat with him. Honestly, when he gave me everything else he had in the world, I thought I might have that too, along with the rest.'
ground_truth_moral: Beware those who value their gain more than their love.

summaries.direct_moral: Excessive attachment to a person's possessions clouds true emotional distress.
summaries.cot_proverb: The desire for what is lost blinds one to the true sorrow of the parting.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Attachment to an object or relationship leads to excessive distress when that object is removed.

fable_text: What a dust I raise! said the Fly on the Coach Wheel. And what a speed I drive at, said the same Fly again on the Horse's Back.
ground_truth_moral: Arrogant fools have no true influence.

summaries.direct_moral: Excessive boasting about one's abilities often masks a lack of true substance.
summaries.cot_proverb: "Pride precedes a fall."
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Hubris leads to self-aggrandizement and a failure to recognize the limits of one's own power.

fable_text: You and I are so alike, says the Eel to the Snake, that I think we should be somewhat related; and yet those who hunt me are afraid of you. What could be the reason for this? Oh, says the Snake, because nobody harms me without paying for it.
ground_truth_moral: In all disputes, those who keep their opponents afraid of revenge come out best.

summaries.direct_moral: Those who hunt the Eel fear the Snake because the Snake demands payment for harm done.
summaries.cot_proverb: "The one who can exact revenge is often feared more than the one who merely seeks it."
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Reciprocity in relationships is often confused with mutual dependence or shared vulnerability.

fable_text: A donkey was complaining that he lacked horns, while a monkey was complaining that she lacked a tail. The mole said to them, 'Silence your complaining, since you can see that I am deprived of eyes.'
ground_truth_moral: Men would bear their misfortunes more patiently if they considered the ones of others.

summaries.direct_moral: Do not focus on your own lack, for others may suffer more profound privations.
summaries.cot_proverb: Silence your complaining, since you can see that I am deprived of eyes.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The superficial complaints of individuals mask a deeper, unaddressed deficiency in others, which is ignored by a third party who possesses a more profound lack.

fable_text: Once, a terrible storm blew at sea, and one sailor noticed that the rest of his companions were each praying to different saints. 'Be careful, my friends,' he said, 'what if we all drown before the messenger can deliver our message? Wouldn't it be better to pray directly to the one who can save us without any help?' With that, they turned their prayers to God himself, and the wind soon calmed.
ground_truth_moral: The shortest and surest way of doing things is best.

summaries.direct_moral: Unity in purpose overcomes division in method.
summaries.cot_proverb: The surest way to find salvation is to seek a unified source of help rather than fragmented reliance on diverse intermediaries.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: True wisdom lies in seeking unified reliance when facing shared peril.

fable_text: A Cook was frying a Dish of Live Fish, and as soon as they felt the Heat of the Pan, one cried out, 'We can't stand this!' and they all jumped into the Fire. Instead of making things better, they were worse off than before.
ground_truth_moral: When we are avoiding present dangers, we should not fall into even worse peril.

summaries.direct_moral: Excessive passion or fear can lead to a worse outcome than the original situation.
summaries.cot_proverb: Moral: Fear only compounds misfortune.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The danger of an overwhelming experience leads to a destructive, panicked response that exacerbates the initial problem rather than resolving it.

fable_text: The Beasts formed an alliance with the Fishes against the Birds. War was declared; but the Fishes, instead of sending their share, sent their excuse, saying they could not travel by land.
ground_truth_moral: An alliance with the powerless is futile.

summaries.direct_moral: Inability to meet a shared obligation excuses a failure to cooperate.
summaries.cot_proverb: The excuse of inability is often a shield for dereliction of duty.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The inability to fulfill a commitment due to an insurmountable constraint reveals that self-interest can mask a lack of genuine solidarity when faced with inconvenient limitations.

fable_text: An Ambassador, constantly bothered by drums and trumpets during his journey, wanted to save money. So, he kept telling them that he was in deep mourning for his mother and not in the mood for music. The drums and trumpets were as upset by this news as the Ambassador himself. A nobleman heard about this and paid a visit to express his condolences. 'My Lord,' asked the nobleman, 'how long has your mother been dead?' 'Oh,' replied the Ambassador, 'it's been about forty years now.' This explained the situation and ended the matter.
ground_truth_moral: There is a clever way of joking between seriousness and jest that brings both enjoyment and benefit.

summaries.direct_moral: A clever excuse can resolve a difficult situation.
summaries.cot_proverb: A reputation for sorrow can mask a deeper truth.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Truth can be obscured by self-serving pretense.

fable_text: A clever gentleman, upon hearing that an old friend of his had been promoted to Cardinal, went to congratulate him on his new honor. 'Please, sir,' said the Cardinal, looking at him oddly, 'may I have your name and know your business?' 'I have come,' said the gentleman, 'to express my sympathy and to tell you how much I pity those burdened with high rank and position; for it affects their minds so much that they can no longer see, hear, or understand like other men, and makes them completely forget their old friends, as if they had never met them before.'
ground_truth_moral: Honors change behavior.

summaries.direct_moral: True sympathy is often masked by a desire to isolate oneself from genuine connection.
summaries.cot_proverb: The higher one rises, the more isolated one becomes from those they once knew.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The superficiality of status obscures genuine human connection.

fable_text: A mocking young rascal happened to meet an old man, whose age and weakness had bent his body like a bow. 'Please, Father,' he said, 'will you sell your bow?' 'Save your money, you fool,' replied the old man, 'for when you reach my age, you'll have such a bow for free.'
ground_truth_moral: Do not mock the feelings of old age, as no one can escape them if they go on living.

summaries.direct_moral: Youthful mockery often underestimates the value of experience and future opportunity.
summaries.cot_proverb: The moral is that one should not underestimate the value of age or experience.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Delayed gratification and the wisdom of patience yield greater rewards than immediate, shallow gain.

fable_text: There was a serious old man who lived to about seventy years without ever knowing the difference between a woman and a weather vane. The devil owed him a trick and repaid him by making the old fool marry a young girl. He often complained afterward about how poorly he had spent his time. 'When I was a young man,' he said, 'I wanted a wife, and now that I'm an old man, my wife wants a husband.'
ground_truth_moral: All things are to be done in their own time.

summaries.direct_moral: Experience and desire are often misplaced when one fails to discern true value.
summaries.cot_proverb: "When the season changes, so does the heart's true direction."
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of desired relationships leads to a cycle of unmet expectations and misplaced desires.

fable_text: There was a lively and confident Magpie who thought the birds would never be well led until he himself was in charge. With this idea, he asked the Eagle to make him part of his council. 'I have the right look for it,' he said. 'I can talk and move quickly, and I can make as much noise and fuss as anyone with wings.' He was about to continue listing his skills when the Eagle kindly interrupted him. 'I know how well you can talk and how lively you are,' said the Eagle, 'but you chatter so much that people might think I've chosen a clown for a Prime Minister.'
ground_truth_moral: Big talkers are not suited for trust, business, or conversation.

summaries.direct_moral: Excessive boasting can lead to being underestimated.
summaries.cot_proverb: The loudest voice does not guarantee the wisest ear.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Arrogance in self-perception leads to misjudgment by those with greater authority.

fable_text: It's common when a strange Dog passes through a Town for a hundred Curs to bark at his tail, and every little Dog tries to bite him. There was one Dog who, seeing he couldn't save himself by running, turned on his Chasers, and found that one set of Teeth was better than two fast Legs; for when he did that, they all backed off and slunk away. A Captain used this example for his Soldiers. Fellow-Soldiers, he said, remember this: Those who run away are in more danger than those who stand and face the fight.
ground_truth_moral: Putting up with one insult invites another.

summaries.direct_moral: Courage in confrontation is superior to flight in peril.
summaries.cot_proverb: The greater danger lies in fleeing from conflict than in confronting it directly.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Courage is demonstrated not by evasion but by resolute confrontation.

fable_text: Whenever through their own fault something bad happens to them, people in general put the blame either on luck or on the devil; in fact, everybody acts this way on their own behalf in order to shed the blame. The devil doesn't like this! It happened that he noticed an old woman climbing a tree, he could anticipate that she was going to fall out of the tree and that she would put the blame on him. So he summoned witnesses and said: 'Look at that old woman climbing the tree, not at my recommendation; I can tell she is going to fall out of that tree. So you be my witnesses that I did not persuade her to climb up there in her shoes.' Soon encough the old woman fell down and when she was asked why she had climbed a tree wearing her shoes she said, 'The devil made me do it.' Then the devil called forward his witnesses and provided that this thing had been done by the old woman not at his recommendation.
ground_truth_moral: Only untrustworthy people blame the consequences of their own actions on others.

summaries.direct_moral: Blaming others for one's own failings is merely a way to evade personal responsibility.
summaries.cot_proverb: When one fails, it is easier to blame external forces than to accept personal fault.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Blame-shifting serves only to evade personal responsibility.

fable_text: As a group of Frogs were playing and jumping around in a Meadow, some Tortoises watching them felt upset that they couldn't do the same. But after a while, they noticed how these Frogs were caught and eaten by Birds and Fishes. One of the Tortoises said, 'It's better to live slow and steady than to die quick and lively.'
ground_truth_moral: Embrace your own strengths.

summaries.direct_moral: Patience and steadiness offer a better chance for survival than reckless speed.
summaries.cot_proverb: Slow and steady wins the race.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Prudence in pace ensures longevity over fleeting exuberance.

fable_text: The Mice found it so troublesome to keep climbing the Oak for every bite they ate, that they decided to use their teeth to bring the Acorns down to them. But some were wiser than others, and an experienced old Mouse warned them to be careful. 'If we destroy our provider now, who will feed us later?' he said.
ground_truth_moral: Consider the consequences of your actions.

summaries.direct_moral: Dependence on a provider can lead to self-destructive short-term gains.
summaries.cot_proverb: If we destroy our provider now, who will feed us later?
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Prudence in present action ensures the sustainability of future well-being.

fable_text: There was a bob-tailed dog advertised in a newspaper, and someone who recognized him by his markings brought him back to his owner. The owner began to question the dog, asking how he could be so ungrateful and insensitive to run away from someone who was so kind to him. 'Did I ever hit you,' he asked, 'or even speak a harsh word to you in all the time you served me?' 'No,' replied the dog, 'not with your own hands or words, but you have had others do it for you many times. When I am beaten by your order, I consider it as if you yourself are beating me.'
ground_truth_moral: What a person does through another is truly their own action.

summaries.direct_moral: True gratitude is shown not just by direct harm, but by the consistent kindness and respect shown in daily interactions.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true measure of kindness is in the manner of its expression, for even gentle treatment can be perceived as harsh when it is imposed by authority.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Dependence breeds a sense of entitlement and a failure to recognize the source of kindness.

fable_text: The Birds were once in a terrible fright, fearing gunshots from the Beetles. The reason was that the little balls of dirt the Beetles had gathered were mistaken by the Birds for bullets. But a Sparrow in the group, who was wiser than the others, told them to take heart. 'How can they reach us in the air,' he said, 'with those pellets they can barely roll on the ground?'
ground_truth_moral: Many people fear danger where there is none, and feel safe where there is danger: put things in perspective.

summaries.direct_moral: Wisdom can overcome fear by questioning the perceived threat.
summaries.cot_proverb: The wisest individual's insight can overcome collective fear based on misperception.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Wisdom in perspective can overcome fear based on misperception.

fable_text: A Bear was once so angry at being stung by a Bee that he rushed into the Bee-Garden and knocked over all the Hives in revenge. This act brought out swarms of Bees against him, and he later realized it would have been wiser to ignore one sting than to provoke a thousand with his pointless anger.
ground_truth_moral: It's better to ignore an insult from one troublemaker than to bring a whole crowd of trouble upon yourself.

summaries.direct_moral: Pointless anger invites greater retribution than restraint.
summaries.cot_proverb: A stitch in time saves nine.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Excessive anger leads to self-destructive and disproportionate consequences.

fable_text: A bird catcher who had set up his net and bait, sat in his spot to watch for a catch. Many birds came one after another, landing and pecking for a while before flying away again. They kept coming and going all day long, but so few at a time that the man didn't think it was worth catching them. Finally, after missing all his chances hoping for a bigger catch, evening came and the birds went to sleep. He had to either pull the net then or not at all, and in the end, he had to be satisfied with just one chaffinch that was unlucky enough to be out later than the others.
ground_truth_moral: Do not trade what might come, which is uncertain, for present opportunities, which can never be regained.

summaries.direct_moral: Persistence in seeking a large reward often yields only meager, accidental success.
summaries.cot_proverb: The pursuit of large rewards often leads to the neglect of small, consistent gains.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of large rewards often leads to the neglect of small, consistent gains.

fable_text: A Soldier who already had one excellent horse bought another that was not nearly as good, yet he took more care of the new one than the first. Everyone wondered at this, since the new horse was neither as beautiful nor as useful as the other. 'Ah,' said someone, 'it's natural to be fond of the newest arrival.'
ground_truth_moral: Everything pleases at first, but nothing pleases for long.

summaries.direct_moral: Excessive attachment to novelty often overshadows the value of established quality.
summaries.cot_proverb: The newer possession is often favored over the established one, regardless of its merit.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of novelty can blind judgment regarding inherent value.

fable_text: I wonder, said a Sow to a Spaniel, how you can be so loyal to a Master who gives you so many blows and pulls your ears. Well, said the Dog, but then consider the good food and kind words he gives me, and I'm better off for it.
ground_truth_moral: We should not be upset by the words of our teachers, since those words are usually the cause of many good things.

summaries.direct_moral: Loyalty is often motivated by the rewards of kindness.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true measure of a relationship is found in the balance of suffering and reward.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Loyalty is often motivated by the perceived balance of rewards and punishments.

fable_text: Why don't you hurry up? cried the Timber in the Cart to the Oxen pulling it. The load isn't that heavy, surely. Well! said the Oxen, if you knew your own fate, you wouldn't be so cheerful about ours. We will be free of our load as soon as we reach our journey's end, but you, destined to be beams and supports, will have to bear weight until you break. This made the Timber understand the situation better.
ground_truth_moral: It's wise and kind to be considerate of one another, for no one knows their own end.

summaries.direct_moral: Do not underestimate the burdens others carry.
summaries.cot_proverb: The destined to bear the burden will suffer the most, even when others are free.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The unequal distribution of burdens reveals that individuals often assume their own burdens are less significant than those of others, only to discover that their own destined suffering or obligation is equally, if not more, severe.

fable_text: A Goldfinch escaped from his Master's Cage, and the Master tried to get him back, but the bird refused to return. 'Well!' said the Boy, 'you'll regret it, because you'll never be as well cared for anywhere else.' 'That may be true,' said the Bird, 'but I would rather take care of myself than be under your care.'
ground_truth_moral: There is no wealth greater than liberty.

summaries.direct_moral: Self-reliance is preferable to a compromised dependency.
summaries.cot_proverb: The strength of self-reliance over the comfort of dependence.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Autonomy necessitates the assertion of self-interest over the comfort of dependence.

fable_text: Practically all the birds were invited to the Eagle's wedding. They resented the fact that the Hoopoe had been given a higher seat of honor than the other birds because the Hoopoe was notable for its crown and for being adorned with multicolored feathers, although it was accustomed to spend all its time rolling around in dung and filth.
ground_truth_moral: True worth should be measured by character and deeds, not by outward appearances.

summaries.direct_moral: True worth is not determined by outward appearance or past habits.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true measure of worth is not in outward appearance or superficial accomplishments, but in the recognition of inherent, albeit unconventional, value.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: True worth is not determined by outward appearance or superficial attributes but by the recognition of inherent, albeit unconventional, value.

fable_text: There was a certain gluttonous priest who had set out on a journey far from home to a wedding to which he had been invited. On the way he found a heap of pears, but he didn't touch so much as a single one of them, although he was feeling very hungry; rather, he made a mockery of them and sprinkled them with piss, for he resented that food of this sort was offered to him on his journey when he was on his way to a splendid wedding feast. But when along the way he ran into a certain river, it was so swollen with rain that he could not cross it without losing his life, and so he decided to go back home. On the way home, starving, he was oppressed by such great hunger that if he had not eaten those very pears, on which he had pissed, he would have died.
ground_truth_moral: Nothing should be condemned, since there is nothing so worthless and humble that it cannot at some time or another prove to be useful.

summaries.direct_moral: Greed and insensitivity can lead to dire consequences.
summaries.cot_proverb: The cost of excessive greed or spiteful resentment can lead to unforeseen and dire consequences.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Excessive desire for immediate gratification, when coupled with a lack of foresight, leads to catastrophic consequences.

fable_text: A Hog noticed a Horse in the peak of his bravery, just about to charge at an Enemy. 'Why, what a fool you are,' said the Hog to him, 'to rush so quickly to your own destruction?' 'That thought,' said the Horse, 'might suit a wicked creature like you, who is only fattened up to be killed by a knife. But when I'm gone, I'll leave behind the memory of a good name.'
ground_truth_moral: It is more honorable to die having achieved noble deeds than to live a life of vice.

summaries.direct_moral: True courage is measured by lasting reputation rather than reckless action.
summaries.cot_proverb: The greater glory of a fleeting, courageous act outweighs the perceived safety of self-preservation.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Self-preservation and reputation are often prioritized over immediate, reckless action.

fable_text: A Tanner bought a Bear-Skin from a Hunter and paid him cash for it. The Hunter told him he would kill a Bear the next day and the Tanner could have the Skin. Out of curiosity, the Tanner went with the Hunter to the Hunt and climbed a tree to watch. The Hunter bravely approached the Bear's Den and sent his Dogs in after it. The Bear rushed out immediately, and the Hunter missed his shot, so the Bear knocked him over. The Hunter held his breath and lay perfectly still, pretending to be dead. The Bear sniffed him, thought he was a corpse, and left him alone. Once the Bear was gone and the danger had passed, the Tanner came down from the tree and told the Hunter to get up. 'Listen, my Friend,' said the Tanner, 'the Bear whispered something in your ear. What was it, I ask?' 'Oh,' said the Hunter, 'he told me to be careful in the future and make sure to catch the Bear before selling its Skin.'
ground_truth_moral: Don't promise more than you can deliver.

summaries.direct_moral: Greed and rash curiosity can lead to unforeseen danger.
summaries.cot_proverb: The danger of greed or overreaching ambition leads to unforeseen consequences, even when one attempts to manipulate or exploit another.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Trusting external actions without understanding the underlying context can lead to unforeseen consequences.

fable_text: There was a Holy Man who spoke to a Soldier about his line of work, pointing out the dangers, sins, and troubles that come with it. 'For the sake of your soul, sir, please give it up,' he said. 'Well, Father,' replied the Soldier, 'I'll do as you say; because we are paid so poorly, and there's so little to gain from plundering, I think I might as well turn to a godly life.'
ground_truth_moral: Many people renounce wicked activities only because they are prevented from conducting them any longer.

summaries.direct_moral: Desperation can motivate a person to abandon a livelihood for a perceived moral life.
summaries.cot_proverb: The lure of ease and spiritual salvation can override practical necessity and personal gain.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Self-interest can undermine moral conviction when external incentives outweigh internal values.

fable_text: A certain man, after the death of his wife whom he had greatly loved, married another woman who was herself a widow. She continuously prattled to him about the virtues and great deeds of her late husband. The man, in order to give tit for tat, also talked all the time about the excellent character and remarkable good wisdom of his late wife. One day, however, the woman grew angry and gave to a poor man who was begging alms part of the chicken she had roasted for their dinner, telling the beggar: I give this to you on behalf of the soul of my previous husband. When her spouse heard this, he summoned the beggar and gave him the other half of the chicken, saying: I also give this to you, for the soul of my late wife. In this way, the two of them, as they wanted to injure one another, ended up not having anything to eat for dinner.
ground_truth_moral: Do not fight with those who are perfectly capable of fighting back.

summaries.direct_moral: Mutual malice only leads to mutual ruin.
summaries.cot_proverb: The greater the desire for revenge or mutual slight, the more destructive it is to all involved.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of mutual grievance ultimately destroys both parties by focusing on past affections rather than present needs.

fable_text: A Lion found himself trapped in a Net and called to a Mouse passing by to help him out, promising never to forget the kindness. The Mouse gnawed the threads apart, and after freeing the Lion, asked in return for his Daughter's hand in marriage. The Lion, being generous, could not refuse. But unfortunately, as the new Bride was about to step into the Marriage Bed, she accidentally stepped on her Husband and crushed him to death.
ground_truth_moral: Unequal matches bring misery.

summaries.direct_moral: Kindness repaid with greed often leads to unforeseen and tragic consequences.
summaries.cot_proverb: The greatest kindness repaid with greed can lead to unforeseen and devastating consequences.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Selfless acts of kindness can invite unforeseen and destructive consequences.

fable_text: There was once a debate about Wax and Brick, questioning why Wax was so fragile and easily broken, while Brick could withstand all damage and weather, remaining strong and firm. The Wax thought about this and concluded that it was the burning that made the Brick hard. So, it threw itself into the fire, believing that heat would harden the Wax too. But what made the Brick solid, melted the Wax.
ground_truth_moral: It's foolish to make decisions without understanding the nature of the matter at hand.

summaries.direct_moral: Believing that hardship would strengthen him, the fragile sought destruction, only to find that the very thing he sought to overcome was what ultimately destroyed him.
summaries.cot_proverb: The heat that destroys one thing can be the very force that destroys another, often without realizing the true nature of the relationship between the elements.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of understanding external causes for misfortune often leads to self-destructive actions, while the true nature of a situation is revealed by an unforeseen interaction.

fable_text: 'Oh, the endless misery of the life I lead!' cries the hard-working farmer, 'spending all my days plowing, sowing, digging, and fertilizing, and in the end, gaining nothing from it! Now, in a soldier's life, there's honor to be earned, and one lucky strike can set a man up forever. Indeed, I'll sell my stock, get a horse and arms, and try my luck in war'. Off he goes, makes his attempt, faces the battle, and ends up leaving a leg or an arm behind to return home. By this time, he's had enough of being a knight-errant, and a new idea strikes him. He thinks he might do better as a merchant. No sooner does this thought excite him than he gets a ship, loads it, and sets off to sea on an adventure: building castles in the air and imagining both the Indies in his coffers before he's even left the port. Well! And what's the end of all this? He encounters bad weather, among shoals and rocks, where merchant, vessel, goods, and all are lost in one common wreck.
ground_truth_moral: Everyone should be satisfied with their own lot in life, given that disaster awaits us on every side.

summaries.direct_moral: Blindly pursuing fleeting fortunes without sound judgment leads to ruin.
summaries.cot_proverb: "A change of endeavor does not guarantee success."
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of success through various endeavors, lacking proper focus or understanding of the underlying risks, leads to repeated failure.

fable_text: A donkey was frustrated that a certain buffoon was given a public office and was wrapped in elegant clothes because he was able to make loud noises come out of his gut. So the donkey went to the city officials, asking that they would honor him no less than they had done the buffoon. When the city officials, surprised at the donkey's request, asked him why he considered himself worthy of such honor, the donkey said: 'Because I emit even louder farts than the buffoon does, and what's more they don't stink.'
ground_truth_moral: When public officials encourage foolishness, it's no surprise if fools try to become public officials.

summaries.direct_moral: Humility is demonstrated by recognizing one's own lesser faults.
summaries.cot_proverb: The more a creature boasts of its unique defect, the less it is truly valued.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of validation through comparison leads to a misguided attempt to assert superiority based on a flawed or irrelevant metric, ultimately revealing that self-proclaimed superiority is often rooted in superficial or ineffectual claims rather than genuine merit.

fable_text: There was a dispute between a River and a Fountain about which of the two should be preferred. The River boasted about the abundance and variety of fish it produced, the benefits of navigation, and the many grand towns and palaces built along its banks for the pleasure of the location. It claimed to provide great satisfaction to mankind in terms of both convenience and delight. Meanwhile, the Fountain (said the River) passed obscurely through the earth's caverns, lay buried in moss, and crept into the world as if ashamed to show its head. The Fountain was so offended by this arrogance and vanity that it stopped its spring and ceased the flow of its waters. As a result, the River's channel dried up, leaving the fish dead and rotting in the mud.
ground_truth_moral: He who claims any good for himself takes away from the source of all the good he enjoys.

summaries.direct_moral: Arrogance in boasting leads to the loss of true value.
summaries.cot_proverb: Vanity breeds ruin.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Arrogance in claiming superiority leads to the destruction of one's own prosperity.

fable_text: A notorious criminal, who had committed countless crimes and been through many jails, made a friend of the Devil to help him out of all his troubles. This friend helped him many times, but each time he was caught again, he turned to the Devil for help once more. But on his last call, the Devil came to him with a big bag of old shoes on his back and said plainly, 'Friend, I'm at the end of my line and can help you no longer. I have worn out all these shoes in your service and have not a penny left to buy more. So you must excuse me if I leave you here.'
ground_truth_moral: We should not expect that our crimes will always go unpunished.

summaries.direct_moral: Dependence on evil ultimately leads to utter abandonment when its utility is exhausted.
summaries.cot_proverb: The cost of reliance and the futility of seeking endless favors.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Dependence breeds further exploitation.

fable_text: he birds were deliberating about whether they should elect additional kings, since the eagle alone was not able to rule so many flocks of birds. They would have voted in favor, except that at the warning of the crow they stopped short of adopting the plan: for when the crow was asked for what reason she did not think that more kings ought to be elected, she said, 'Because it is more effort for many sacks to be filled than just one.'
ground_truth_moral: It is better by far to be governed by one chief, than by many.

summaries.direct_moral: Dividing a task among many is more taxing than completing it with a single leader.
summaries.cot_proverb: The cost of division outweighs the benefit of collective effort.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The cost of collective action outweighs the benefit of individual success.

fable_text: A Poor Woman was nearly out of her mind with fear of losing her Husband. The Good Man was Sick and given up on, and nothing would do but that Death must take her instead of him. She called and prayed, and prayed and called, until at last Death appeared in a terrible form at her side. She politely dropped him a curtsy: 'And please, Sir,' she said, 'do not mistake yourself; for the person you came for is lying in the bed there.'
ground_truth_moral: No one is so loving of a friend that they would not prefer their own good to that of another.

summaries.direct_moral: Desperation can mask a lack of true understanding.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true object of one's desire is often mistaken for something else, leading to misplaced devotion and ultimate disappointment.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The futility of desperate, misplaced hope leads to a final, stark confrontation with reality.

fable_text: There was a good man who followed his wife's body to the grave, weeping and wailing all the way, while his son followed the coffin singing. 'Why, you rascal,' said the father, 'you should howl and wring your hands and do as I do, not go singing like a madman.' 'But father,' said the son, 'you pay the priests to sing, so why be angry with me for giving you a song for free?' 'Well,' said the father, 'what suits the priests doesn't always suit you. It's their job to sing, but it's your part to cry.'
ground_truth_moral: Not all things are suitable for all people.

summaries.direct_moral: True devotion is not always matched by the same intensity or method.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true measure of devotion is in the sincerity of one's grief, not in the performance of emotion.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The proper expression of grief and devotion is relative to the individual's role and the nature of the loss.

fable_text: A certain man, German by birth, and exceedingly rich, was ailing. A large number of doctors had come to cure him (flies do indeed come swarming in crowds to honey), and one of them said among other things that the man needed an enema if he wanted to get well. When the man, who was unfamiliar with this medical procedure, heard what the doctor said, he flew into a rage and ordered that the doctors be thrown out of his house, saying that they must be insane since they wanted to cure his butt when it was his head that hurt.
ground_truth_moral: Even beneficial things all appear to be drastic and sure to hurt for those those who are unfamiliar with them and inexperienced.

summaries.direct_moral: Arrogance blinds one to necessary knowledge, leading to destructive rejection of helpful guidance.
summaries.cot_proverb: The arrogance of ignorance.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The arrogance of ignorance leads to the rejection of necessary knowledge.

fable_text: There was a donkey who was ill, and the story went out that he was soon going to die, so when the wolves and the dogs came to visit him and asked his son how his father was feeling, the son replied through a crack in the door: 'Better than you wish.'
ground_truth_moral: Many pretend to be concerned about some people's demise, when however what they want is for it to come quickly.

summaries.direct_moral: True strength lies in inner resilience despite outward appearances of despair.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true measure of a person's spirit is found in their inner resilience, even when facing dire circumstances.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Compassion can mask underlying suffering.

fable_text: A donkey was suffering terribly during the winter time because he was affected by the extreme cold and he had tough chaff fodder. Therefore, he was hoping for the moderation of spring and its tender grasses. But when spring came, he was compelled by his master (who was a potter) to carry the clay into the work area, to carry wood for the oven, and from there to carry the bricks, tiles and roof-tiles to various places. Completely tired of spring, in which season he had to endure so much hard work, he was asking in all his prayers for summer to come, so that his master would be busy with the harvest and they would allow him to rest. But then too, when he was compelled to carry the harvest to the threshing-floor and from there to carry the wheat into the house, and there was not place of rest for him, the donkey was hoping that at least autumn would be an end of his labors. But not even at that time did the donkey see an end of his troubles, since every day there was wood, apples and wine to be carried, so again he begged for the snow and ice of winter, so that at least there would be some rest allotted to him from so much hard work.
ground_truth_moral: Every Season has its own work.

summaries.direct_moral: Constant hardship breeds a desire for relief, yet relentless labor often finds no respite.
summaries.cot_proverb: The relentless cycle of labor leaves no respite.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The relentless cycle of labor and obligation strips away any hope for relief, regardless of the season.

fable_text: As a group of mice were peeking out of their holes to see what was around, they spotted a cat on a shelf. The cat looked so calm and harmless, as if she had no life or spirit in her. 'Well,' said one of the mice, 'that's a kind creature, I'm sure of it. You can see it in her face, and I really want to get to know her.' No sooner said than done; but as soon as the cat had the mouse within reach, she torn her to pieces.
ground_truth_moral: People are to be judged by their actions, not by their appearance.

summaries.direct_moral: Blind admiration for a dangerous creature invites destruction.
summaries.cot_proverb: Beware the creature whose apparent calm masks a destructive inner force.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The danger of assuming benign appearances masks underlying, destructive reality.

fable_text: The Porcupine and the Hedgehog are quite similar, but the Porcupine has longer and sharper quills, which he can shoot at an enemy. A Wolf wanted to deal with him, but first needed to disarm him. So, the Wolf told the Porcupine in a friendly manner that it didn't look good to be armed during peaceful times, as if they were at war. He advised the Porcupine to put his quills aside, saying he could pick them up whenever he wanted. The Porcupine replied, 'You talk of peace? Well, my situation is one of war, and that's why I keep my quills ready as long as a Wolf is around.'
ground_truth_moral: No one can be safe in peace unless they are always ready to face an enemy in case of war.

summaries.direct_moral: A creature must defend itself when threatened, even if it means appearing aggressive.
summaries.cot_proverb: "When in doubt, the prepared defense is mistaken for hostility."
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The principle illustrated is that genuine trust is often impossible when fundamental needs for security are in conflict.

fable_text: A Simple Mouse happened to be nearby when a Kite got caught in a Net. The Kite begged the Mouse to help her get free. The Mouse gnawed a Hole in the Net and set the Kite free; and the Kite ate the Mouse for her trouble.
ground_truth_moral: Save a Thief from the Gallows, and he'll cut your Throat.

summaries.direct_moral: Kindness shown in distress can be repaid with betrayal.
summaries.cot_proverb: The cost of kindness can be repaid with betrayal.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Selfless aid is often met with reciprocal exploitation.

fable_text: In old times, when Jupiter was in the mood to grant requests, a Cockle asked that his house and body be all one piece. Jupiter replied that it would be a burden rather than a favor. 'Yes', said the Cockle, 'but it is a burden I would rather bear than live exposed to bad neighbors.'
ground_truth_moral: Unwanted visits are a nuisance to a peaceful person's life.

summaries.direct_moral: A desire for complete unity can be justified if it prevents greater hardship.
summaries.cot_proverb: The greater good is sometimes found in enduring hardship rather than facing social isolation.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Self-imposed constraint is preferable to external vulnerability.

fable_text: A Snake was convinced during a Cold Winter to let a Hedgehog into his Den. But once inside, the Hedgehog's spines were very uncomfortable for the Snake. So the Snake told him he must find another place, as the Den was too small for both of them. The Hedgehog replied, 'If you can't stay, you should leave. As for me, I'm quite happy here, and if you're not, you're free to go.'
ground_truth_moral: Possession is nine-tenths of the law.

summaries.direct_moral: A creature should leave a situation that causes discomfort for another.
summaries.cot_proverb: If you cannot find a place to fit, you should find one that suits you.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Mutual consideration requires recognizing the limits of one's own desires and adapting to the needs of another.

fable_text: A Fox and a Hare were once in a heated contest about which of them could manage best in the world. 'When I am chased,' said the Hare, 'I can show the dogs a clean pair of heels and run away from them easily.' 'And yet,' said the Fox, 'I have outsmarted more of them with my tricks and cunning than you ever did with your speed.'
ground_truth_moral: Wisdom is far superior to strength.

summaries.direct_moral: Cunning often surpasses mere speed in achieving success.
summaries.cot_proverb: "Cleverness often triumphs over brute force."
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Skill in deception proves more effective than mere physical ability.

fable_text: There was an old man, who, even in his frailty, still had thoughts of women. His spiritual advisor noticed this and gave him earnest advice about the desires of the flesh. The old man thanked him sincerely for his kind and Christian counsel, saying, 'With Heaven's help, I'll follow it. To be honest, I'm told it's not good for me, and truly, my body is no longer fit for such activities.'
ground_truth_moral: Many people give up their vices not out of a love for virtue, but because they can no longer continue indulging in them.

summaries.direct_moral: Excessive desire must be tempered by wisdom and self-awareness.
summaries.cot_proverb: The body's limitations do not negate the persistent nature of desire.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Self-deception in the pursuit of forbidden desires is often masked by a veneer of piety or acceptance.

fable_text: A certain impertinent, thoughtless fellow decided to visit a philosopher. He found him alone in his study and wondered how he could bear to live such a solitary life. The learned man told him, 'Sir, you are very mistaken; I was in very good company until you came in.'
ground_truth_moral: Better to be alone than in bad company.

summaries.direct_moral: True wisdom is often found in the company of others.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true measure of a person is not in their solitude, but in the quality of their relationships.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The danger of judging others based on limited perception is that it can lead to incorrect assumptions about the complexity of another's life or circumstances.

fable_text: There is a story of a Wolf that wrapped himself in a sheep's skin and attacked lambs for a while under that disguise. But the shepherd eventually caught him and hung him up, sheep's skin and all, on a high gallows as a warning and example. The neighbors were amazed and asked why he hung up his sheep. 'Oh,' he said, 'that's just the skin of a sheep that was used to cover the heart, malice, and body of a wolf that hid under it.'
ground_truth_moral: People should not be judged by their appearances, but by their actions.

summaries.direct_moral: Deception, when exposed, reveals the true nature of malice.
summaries.cot_proverb: The disguise of malice cannot conceal true nature.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Deception, when masked by apparent innocence, ultimately reveals a deeper, more dangerous truth.

fable_text: There was a Widow who had a desire for a Second Husband, and she asked a gossiping Friend of hers for help on how to handle it. 'The truth is,' she said, 'I really want another partner, but people would laugh and point at me, and it would make me miserable.' 'You're a fine Widow,' said the Friend, 'to worry about what people say. Listen to me now. You have a Donkey in your fields; go and have that Donkey painted Green, and then let him be shown around the Country. Do this without any more talk, because talk just wastes time.' So it was done, and for the first four or five days, the Green Donkey had the whole Country following him, men, women, and children, all staring and shouting. In four or five more days, the excitement was over, and the Donkey could travel all day without anyone noticing him. 'Now,' said the helpful Friend, 'a newly married Widow is like a Green Donkey: everyone talks about it for the first few days, and then the story fades away.'
ground_truth_moral: There is no thing so worthy of wonderment which does not cease to be a miracle with the passage of time.

summaries.direct_moral: Unnecessary talk only draws attention, while decisive action can fade into obscurity.
summaries.cot_proverb: The moral is: Talk wastes time.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of external validation through excessive discourse yields fleeting attention, while focused, tangible action achieves a temporary, yet effective, visibility.

fable_text: A Shepherd had one favorite dog that he trusted more than all the others. He fed him by hand and cared for him more than any of the other dogs. This kindness continued for a long time until, when some sheep went missing, he suspected this dog of being unfaithful. After this suspicion, he watched him closely and eventually discovered that this trusted servant was the thief. Once he found out, he had the dog caught and told him to prepare for punishment. 'Oh, Master,' said the dog, 'I am part of your family, and it would be harsh to punish a household member. Turn your anger on the wolves instead, who regularly attack your sheep.' 'No, no,' said the Shepherd, 'I would rather spare forty wolves who make it their business to kill sheep than one sheep-stealing dog trusted with their care. There is some honesty and boldness in the wolves, but the dog's betrayal is the worst kind of treachery.'
ground_truth_moral: An enemy in disguise is much more unforgivable than an open enemy.

summaries.direct_moral: Unwavering trust can be betrayed by the most cherished companion.
summaries.cot_proverb: The greater the betrayal of trust, the more severe the resulting judgment should be.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Trust misplaced in a relationship can lead to devastating consequences when loyalty is tested by suspicion.

fable_text: There was once a Ram who defeated all his fellow rams and became so proud of his victories that he decided to challenge a Bull to a fight. They met, and at the first clash, the Ram was knocked out and lay as if dead. When he came to, he said, 'This is the result of my arrogance and foolishness in challenging an enemy that nature made stronger than me.'
ground_truth_moral: We should not pick fights with people stronger than we are.

summaries.direct_moral: Arrogance leads to defeat when challenging a superior force.
summaries.cot_proverb: The greater one, when blinded by pride, underestimates the true power of nature and others.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Hubris invites downfall when one underestimates inherent power.

fable_text: A certain father, whose son had succumbed to vicious tendencies, was urging the boy at great length that he should quit the way of vices and keep his eye instead on the virtues, which would engender praise and honor for him. The son replied: 'You are wasting your time, Father, in urging me to do these things, for I have heard many a preacher, so to speak, and they urged me to the path of virtue with far more talent than you, but I have still never taken any of their advice!'
ground_truth_moral: Men who are wickedly inclined will not stop their vicious behavior, no matter who urges them to do so.

summaries.direct_moral: True guidance is often ignored when one is too eager for change.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true measure of guidance is often found in the recipient's own conviction rather than the intensity of the urging.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: True guidance is often rejected when the individual is entrenched in their current path, regardless of the wisdom offered.

fable_text: There was an Eagle that took a nest of Rabbits and carried them away to her young. The Mother Rabbit followed her with tears in her eyes, pleading in the name of all the powers that protect the innocent and oppressed, to have mercy on her poor children. But the Eagle, in a fit of pride and anger, tore them to pieces. The Rabbit then gathered all the rabbits together, told her story, and sought advice on revenge. 'Divine justice,' she said, 'will not let such cruel acts go unpunished.' They discussed the matter and all agreed that the only way to repay the Eagle was to undermine the tree where she nested. So they all worked at the roots of the tree, leaving it with so little support that the first strong wind brought it crashing down, nest, Eagles, and all. Some of them were killed by the fall; others were eaten by birds and beasts of prey, and the Rabbit finally had the satisfaction of destroying the Eagle's children in revenge for her own.
ground_truth_moral: It is very unwise, even for the greatest of men, to provoke the least, as they may find a way to enact revenge.

summaries.direct_moral: Prideful cruelty invites a just, albeit destructive, retribution.
summaries.cot_proverb: The price of pride is the destruction of all.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Retribution often breeds further destruction.

fable_text: There was a Master-Pike, who for his size, beauty, and strength, was seen as the Prince of the River. But being the ruler of the freshwater wasn't enough for him; he wanted to rule the sea too. With this ambitious plan, he ventured into the ocean and claimed it as his own. However, a mighty Dolphin took offense at this intrusion and chased the Pike back to the edge of his own stream, barely allowing him to escape. From then on, the Pike wisely stayed within his own territory.
ground_truth_moral: Be content with your lot.

summaries.direct_moral: Ambition beyond one's rightful domain invites conflict and eventual retreat.
summaries.cot_proverb: Ambition without rightful authority leads to conflict and retreat.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Ambition without rightful claim leads to conflict and retreat.

fable_text: A Sheep that was about to be sheared was upset with the Shepherd for not being content with the Milk she provided, but also taking her Wool. The Shepherd, without further words, angrily took one of the Lambs and killed it. 'Well,' said the Sheep, 'I hope you've done your worst now.' 'No,' said the Shepherd, 'when that's done, I can cut your throat too if I want, and throw you to the Dogs or Wolves as I please.' The Sheep said nothing more, fearing something worse might happen.
ground_truth_moral: When people won't listen to reason through kind means, they must be made to understand through harsh ones.

summaries.direct_moral: Greed and betrayal can lead to severe retribution.
summaries.cot_proverb: When one takes what is given and then threatens the giver, the relationship is poisoned by mutual destruction.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Self-interest often leads to reciprocal harm when trust is violated.

fable_text: The coachman, hearing one of the wheels of his coach creak, was surprised; but more especially when he perceived that it was the worst wheel of the whole set, and which he thought had but little pretense to take such a liberty.
ground_truth_moral: Complaining is the privilege of the weak.

summaries.direct_moral: Hubris invites downfall.
summaries.cot_proverb: The moral is that one should not presume to take liberties with what is inherently flawed or weak.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The danger of overreaching or presuming beyond one's established competence is highlighted by the coachman's reaction to a faulty wheel.

fable_text: There was a generous Rich Man who always had a grand and open table, so he never lacked guests. This man noticed that all sorts of people came to him, and he became curious to see who were true friends and who were just freeloaders. So one day, at a full table, he mentioned a quarrel he had and said he was about to seek satisfaction. He needed a certain number of people to join him, and he was sure they would stand by him with swords in hand.
