Everyone made excuses except for two, whom he counted as his friends, while the rest were just hangers-on.
ground_truth_moral: True friends are hard to come by.

summaries.direct_moral: True friends will support you in times of need, while others will only seek personal gain.
summaries.cot_proverb: A true friend is found in adversity, not in ease.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: True loyalty is revealed by the willingness of individuals to commit to shared adversity or conflict.

fable_text: As a Dog was chasing closely behind a Fox, a Hare suddenly appeared. 'Hold on a moment,' said the Fox, 'and catch that Hare while you can. You've never tasted such a treat in your life. But I'm all spoiled and rotten, and a bite of my flesh would poison you.' The Dog immediately left the Fox and chased after the Hare, but she was too quick for him. When he realized he couldn't catch her, he wisely let her go. The Hare had overheard what happened, and when she met the Fox a few days later, she told him how badly he had treated her. 'Well,' said the Fox, 'if you are upset that I spoke well of you, what would you have done if I had spoken badly?'
ground_truth_moral: A false friend is the worst kind of enemy.

summaries.direct_moral: Beware of those who exploit your vulnerability for their own gain.
summaries.cot_proverb: The danger of exploiting a vulnerable situation for personal gain.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Trust is fragile when self-interest dictates actions, and the pursuit of gain can lead to the abandonment of a flawed pursuit.

fable_text: The rabbit and the fox made their petitions to Jupiter: the fox asked that swiftness of foot be added to her slyness, and the rabbit asked that slyness be added to his swiftness. Jupiter responded to them as follows: 'From the beginning of the world, from my breast I have poured forth gifts to each and every animal very generously; if I were to give one animal all the fits, that would be an injury to the others.'
ground_truth_moral: Be content with your lot.

summaries.direct_moral: Generosity towards diverse talents prevents the imbalance of singular gifts.
summaries.cot_proverb: The moral of the story is that trying to equalize or manipulate inherent differences in nature will ultimately harm the whole.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The inherent nature of an individual is fixed, and attempting to enhance one's natural talents at the expense of others constitutes an unjust claim on the shared bounty.

fable_text: Quite a few horses had been brought to the race track, all adorned with elaborate accoutrements, all except for one, so the other horses made fun of him for being so shabby and unsuited to this type of competition, and they concluded that he would not ever be a winner. But when the time for the running of the race arrived and the trumpet blew the signal to start, all the horses leaped from their starting cages, and only then did it become clear how much the horse who had just been made fun of actually outdistanced the rest of the horses with his speed. When all the others had been left far behind, that horse won the palm of victory.
ground_truth_moral: Appearances can be deceiving.

summaries.direct_moral: True worth is revealed not by outward appearance but by inner ability.
summaries.cot_proverb: The underdog's true ability is revealed only when the moment of truth arrives.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: True worth is often revealed only through performance rather than superficial appearance.

fable_text: A Countryman who was caught up in a lawsuit had a close friend and relative who was a lawyer. He went to him again and again for advice, but the lawyer was always too busy and told him to come back another time. The poor man took a nice, fat kid goat with him on his next visit, and when the lawyer's clerk heard the goat at the door, he let the man in and took him to his master. There, the man explained his case, got the lawyer's opinion, thanked the lawyer for seeing him, and thanked the goat for getting him in, and then went on his way.
ground_truth_moral: Money opens all doors.

summaries.direct_moral: Persistence, coupled with ingenuity, can sometimes overcome inaccessibility.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true value of persistence is often found not in relentless pursuit, but in adapting one's approach to meet the availability of the opportunity.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Trust in persistent, creative effort can sometimes overcome initial inaccessibility.

fable_text: A certain young man had married a woman who was also quite young, very beautiful and lusty. Wanting to satisfy her boundless appetite, his loins had gotten so worn out that after just a few days he had gotten thin and looked more dead than alive: he could not walk, he could not stand up, he could do any kind of work, and so like some kind of old man he took his pleasure in the sun. So when he was enjoying some sunny place, warming himself by the sun's heat, it happened that some hunters, who had been chasing a wolf, came that way. When the young man asked them why they hadn't caught the wolf, they replied, 'We were not able to pursue him because of his incredible swiftness.' 'Well,' he said, 'if my wife were in charge of him, she'd put an end to his running.'
ground_truth_moral: Even the strongest can be brought low by unchecked desires.

summaries.direct_moral: Excessive desire leads to ruin.
summaries.cot_proverb: When one seeks too much, one invites greater peril.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The pursuit of excessive gratification leads to a state of utter depletion, rendering the individual incapable of functioning, which ironically invites a new, perhaps more dangerous, form of attention or consequence.

fable_text: An Old Man caught a Boy stealing apples from his orchard. 'Hey,' he said, 'come down from that tree and stop taking my apples.' The Boy ignored him and kept picking. 'Well,' said the Old Man, 'they say herbs have magic just like words do,' and he threw a handful of grass at the Boy. This was so silly that the Young Thief thought the Old Man was joking. But finally, the Old Man said, 'If words and herbs won't work, I'll try stones, because they say stones have power too,' and with that, he got the job done.
ground_truth_moral: Those who won't learn from advice must be taught by experience.

summaries.direct_moral: Persistence and varied methods will eventually achieve the desired result.
summaries.cot_proverb: The surest way to solve a problem is to employ a variety of methods when initial attempts fail.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Persistence in the face of ineffective methods eventually yields results.

fable_text: As a Nightingale was singing in a bush, down swoops a sneaky Sparrow-Hawk and snatches her from the branch. The poor Nightingale pleaded for her life, saying that her small body wasn't worth it and that there were bigger birds to catch. The Hawk replied, 'Do you think I'm so foolish as to give up a little bird I have for a bigger one I don't have?' Then she offered, 'I'll sing you a lovely song for my life.' 'No, no,' said the Hawk, 'I need food for my belly, not music for my ears.'
ground_truth_moral: A bird in the hand is worth two in the bush.

summaries.direct_moral: True worth is not measured by perceived value, but by immediate need.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true value of something is often judged by its perceived worth rather than its intrinsic quality or the immediate need of the observer.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Self-interest often overrides altruism.

fable_text: A Lion, who found it very bothersome to live alone, let the Beasts of the Forest know that he had decided to choose one of them as a Friend and Companion. There was a great commotion about who would be the Favorite and the envy of all the others. The Lion chose a Hog, saying, 'He is true and faithful to his friend and will stand by him in all times and dangers.'
ground_truth_moral: Seek the friendship of those who do not withdraw from offering help even in a time of adversity.

summaries.direct_moral: True loyalty is found in faithfulness to a companion.
summaries.cot_proverb: A friend is found in loyalty and steadfastness.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Trustworthiness is demonstrated through the commitment to loyalty in friendship.

fable_text: A Gnat, nearly frozen and hungry, went to a Bee-Hive on a cold morning to ask for help. The Gnat offered to teach music to the Bee's family in exchange for food and shelter. The Bee politely declined, saying, 'I teach all my children my own trade so they can earn a living through hard work.'
ground_truth_moral: Educate your children in those arts which are capable of preserving them from want.

summaries.direct_moral: True expertise and self-sufficiency are valued over superficial trade.
summaries.cot_proverb: "Do not offer a trick where you seek gain through false promise."
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Self-sufficiency through earned skill is valued over dependency based on offering an unrelated service.

fable_text: When a war broke out between the Birds and the Beasts, the Lion called all his subjects from ages sixteen to sixty to gather with weapons at a specific time and place, or face his great displeasure. Among those who showed up were many Asses and Hares. Some of the leaders wanted to dismiss them, thinking they were not fit for service. 'Do not be mistaken,' said the Lion, 'the Asses will do well as trumpeters, and the Hares will make excellent letter-carriers.'
ground_truth_moral: No one is so contemptible that he cannot be of use to us in some way.

summaries.direct_moral: Those deemed unfit can find valuable, albeit different, roles in service.
summaries.cot_proverb: The true measure of worth is not in perceived capability but in specialized utility.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The utility of individuals is judged by their perceived capability for a specific, albeit subordinate, role, rather than their inherent worth or potential.

fable_text: Once there was a fierce civil war among the Hawks. The gentle and innocent Pigeons, out of pure kindness, sent their representatives to try and make peace between them. While the Hawks were busy fighting each other, they paid no attention to anything else. But as soon as they made peace, they returned to their old habit of attacking the Pigeons. This made the Pigeons realize their mistake and understand the danger of helping a common enemy, which led to their own downfall.
ground_truth_moral: The conflicts of the wicked are the protection of the good.

summaries.direct_moral: Helping a common enemy can lead to unforeseen and dangerous consequences.
summaries.cot_proverb: Helping an enemy often invites further peril.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Intervention in conflict can invite unforeseen and dangerous repercussions.

fable_text: There is a story that Death visited an Old Man and told him to come along. The Man tried to excuse himself, saying that the journey to the Other World was too long to take on such short notice, and he asked for a little more time to make his Will before he died. Death replied, 'You have had enough warning, one would think, to have been ready by now.' 'In truth,' said the Old Man, 'this is the first time I have ever seen you in my whole life.' 'That's not true,' said Death, 'for you have seen daily examples of mortality before your eyes, in people of all kinds, ages, and ranks. Isn't the frequent sight of others' deaths a reminder enough to make you think of your own? Your dim and hollow eyes, the loss of your hearing, and the faltering of your other senses should remind you, without much ado, that Death has already taken hold of you. And do you think this is the time to keep putting it off? Your final hour, I tell you, has now come, and there's no thought of a reprieve in the case of Fate.'
ground_truth_moral: We should live as if we always saw Death standing by.

summaries.direct_moral: A person's procrastination regarding their end is futile when the time for it has arrived.
summaries.cot_proverb: The moral is that procrastination in facing one's end is futile.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: The necessity of confronting one's own mortality.

fable_text: There was a certain miserly man who was dying and about to part with his enormous heap of money, ill-gotten as it was. He ordered that the bag of coins be brought to him as he was dying and then asked the bag to whom it would bring pleasure. The bag answered him: 'I'm going to bring pleasure to your heirs, who will squander these coins, over which you sweated, on women and drinks, and I'll also bring pleasure to the demons who are going to turn your soul over to eternal torments.'
ground_truth_moral: A life of greed and hoarding wealth leads to misery.

summaries.direct_moral: Greed invites ruin for both the greedy and the damned.
summaries.cot_proverb: The greed for worldly gain often leads to ruin for oneself and one's legacy.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: Selfishness invites ruin for both the self and others.

fable_text: An elm tree sprang up on the bank of a river, and the elm tree mocked the willow tree who was her neighbor for being weak and feeble, as it bent with every surge of the water, no matter how light, while the elm tree praised her own stoutness and strength with extravagant words, because for many years the elm tree had remained unshaken by even the most persistent movement of the stream. On one occasion, however, the elm tree snapped from the tremendous violence of the waves and as she was being dragged along by the waters the willow tree laughed and said: Why are you abandoning me, neighbor? Where is your stoutness now?'
ground_truth_moral: Those who adapt to the times will emerge unscathed.

summaries.direct_moral: Hubris leads to a downfall, regardless of perceived strength.
summaries.cot_proverb: The greater strength is often revealed by the test of adversity.
summaries.conceptual_abstract: True strength is revealed not by boastful endurance but by the capacity for connection and vulnerability.
